Duty of Care — General Principles.front: What is the three-stage Caparo test for establishing a novel duty of care in negligence?
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
The Caparo test (Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] HL) requires:

1. Reasonable foreseeability — the claimant, or a class including the claimant, was foreseeably at risk of damage
2. Proximity — a sufficiently close relationship between claimant and defendant
3. Fair, just and reasonable — it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty in all the circumstances.

All three stages must be satisfied. Per Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] SC, Caparo is only the correct framework in novel situations — courts should first ask whether an established duty category applies.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: CAPARO THREE-STAGE TEST · FORESEEABILITY · PROXIMITY · FAIR JUST REASONABLE · NOVEL DUTY · ROBINSON

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: What did Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] establish, and why is it significant?
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] HL established:

1. The neighbour principle — Lord Atkin, in the majority, held that a duty of care is owed to those so closely and directly affected by your acts that you ought reasonably to have them in contemplation when directing your mind to those acts or omissions
2. A manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer where there is no reasonable possibility of intermediate examination before the product reaches the consumer
3. It laid the foundation for the modern law of negligence by moving beyond specific recognised categories toward a general principle.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: NEIGHBOUR PRINCIPLE · LORD ATKIN (MAJORITY) · MANUFACTURER LIABILITY · NO INTERMEDIATE EXAMINATION · FOUNDATION OF NEGLIGENCE

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: What approach to novel duty of care situations did the Supreme Court confirm in Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018]?
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
In Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] SC, Lord Reed clarified:

1. Where the facts fall within an established duty of care category, a duty exists without needing to apply Caparo
2. Caparo's three-stage test is a tool for truly novel situations only
3. The law develops incrementally, by close analogy with established categories.

This corrected the widespread misunderstanding that Caparo required re-application in every negligence case. Established categories (road users, employers, manufacturers, occupiers) are not to be re-examined through Caparo each time.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: ROBINSON v CHIEF CONSTABLE · INCREMENTAL APPROACH · ESTABLISHED CATEGORIES · CAPARO FOR NOVEL CASES ONLY · LORD REED

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: What duty did the House of Lords find (or decline to find) on the facts of Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990]?
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] HL: Caparo purchased shares in Fidelity plc relying on audited accounts prepared by Dickman. After taking over Fidelity, they found the accounts were wrong. The House of Lords held: the auditors owed no duty of care to members of the public or investors who relied on the accounts to make investment decisions, nor to existing shareholders seeking to acquire further shares. The accounts were prepared for the limited statutory purpose — not for use by individual investors.

**Key principles:**

1. Purpose of information limits the duty
2. A Hedley Byrne assumption of responsibility requires the statement to be made for the specific purpose relied upon.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: CAPARO v DICKMAN · AUDITORS · NO DUTY TO INVESTORS · PURPOSE OF REPORT · LIMITS HEDLEY BYRNE · FORESEEABILITY PROXIMITY FAIR/JUST/REASONABLE

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: When does a court apply the Caparo three-stage test, and when does it NOT? List six established duty categories where Caparo is not applied, and explain the Robinson principle.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: CAPARO NOT UNIVERSAL · ROBINSON PRINCIPLE · ESTABLISHED CATEGORIES · NOVEL SITUATIONS ONLY · SIX CATEGORIES · INCREMENTAL DEVELOPMENT

Duty of Care — General Principles.issue: Whether a manufacturer owed a duty of care in negligence to the ultimate consumer of a product, in the absence of any contractual relationship.
Duty of Care — General Principles.rule: A person owes a duty of care to their 'neighbour' — those so closely and directly affected by their acts that they ought reasonably to have them in contemplation when directing their mind to those acts. A manufacturer owes a duty to the ultimate consumer where there is no reasonable possibility of intermediate examination.
Duty of Care — General Principles.application: Mrs Donoghue consumed ginger beer from an opaque bottle manufactured by Stevenson. A decomposed snail was allegedly found inside. She suffered gastroenteritis. She had no contract with Stevenson (her friend had bought the bottle). The House of Lords held a duty existed in tort despite the absence of contract.
Duty of Care — General Principles.conclusion: Stevenson owed a duty of care to Mrs Donoghue. Lord Atkin's 'neighbour principle' laid the foundations for the modern law of negligence, moving it beyond fixed categories toward a general principle of liability for foreseeable harm.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: NEIGHBOUR PRINCIPLE · LORD ATKIN · MAJORITY · MANUFACTURER → CONSUMER · NO INTERMEDIATE EXAMINATION · FOUNDATION OF NEGLIGENCE

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] HL
Duty of Care — General Principles.issue: Whether auditors owed a duty of care to investors and individual shareholders who relied on statutory accounts when making investment decisions.
Duty of Care — General Principles.rule:
For a duty of care to arise in novel situations, three requirements must be satisfied:

1. reasonable foreseeability of damage
2. proximity between the parties
3. it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty.

A duty of care to prevent pure economic loss from a statement is further limited by the purpose for which the statement was made.
Duty of Care — General Principles.application: Caparo acquired shares in Fidelity plc, relying on audited accounts prepared by Dickman. The accounts proved inaccurate; the company was worth far less than shown. Caparo argued the auditors owed them a duty. The HL held the accounts were prepared for the statutory purpose of enabling existing shareholders as a body to exercise control over the company — not to assist individual investment decisions.
Duty of Care — General Principles.conclusion: No duty of care was owed to Caparo as investors or as individual shareholders buying further shares. The duty was owed to the shareholders as a body for the limited statutory purpose. Caparo established the three-stage test that applies in novel duty of care situations.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: THREE-STAGE TEST · FORESEEABILITY · PROXIMITY · FAIR JUST REASONABLE · AUDITORS · PURPOSE OF ACCOUNTS · NO DUTY TO INVESTORS

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] SC
Duty of Care — General Principles.issue: Whether the police owed a duty of care to a pedestrian injured when officers, while attempting to arrest a drug dealer in a public place, caused him to collide with her.
Duty of Care — General Principles.rule: Where an established duty category covers the facts, Caparo's three-stage test does not need to be re-applied. Courts should first ask whether an established category of duty applies; if so, they should apply it. The Caparo test is reserved for genuinely novel situations. The law develops incrementally by analogy with established categories.
Duty of Care — General Principles.application: Officers attempted to arrest a drug dealer on a busy street. In the process, the dealer collided with an 87-year-old pedestrian, causing her injuries. The HL had previously held in Dorset Yacht and other cases that the police could owe duties in certain circumstances. The SC held this fell within established principles: police officers, like any road user or person acting in public, must take reasonable care not to cause foreseeable physical injury to people nearby.
Duty of Care — General Principles.conclusion: The Chief Constable was liable. Lord Reed confirmed that Caparo is not a universal test applied in every case; established duty categories govern where they apply. The decision corrected the lower courts' misapplication of Caparo to a non-novel situation.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: CAPARO NOT UNIVERSAL · ESTABLISHED CATEGORIES · INCREMENTAL APPROACH · POLICE · PHYSICAL INJURY · LORD REED · NOVEL SITUATIONS ONLY

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: Priya is cycling along a road when Derek, a delivery driver, opens his van door without checking his mirror. The door strikes Priya and she is thrown from her bicycle, breaking her collarbone. Advise Priya on whether Derek owes her a duty of care.
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
Duty of care: This falls within an established duty category — road users owe a duty of care to other road users and those in the vicinity of the road (Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018] SC). The Caparo three-stage test does not need to be re-applied. Derek, as a road user (including when stationary but actively using a van door), owes a duty of care to Priya as a cyclist nearby.

The remaining elements of negligence:

1. Breach — opening a door without checking is a failure to reach the standard of the reasonable driver
2. Causation — but for Derek's breach, Priya would not have been struck (Barnett v Chelsea [1969])
3. Damage — the broken collarbone is physical injury, a foreseeable type of harm (Wagon Mound No 1 [1961]).

Conclusion: Derek owes Priya a duty of care. On these facts, all elements of negligence are likely made out and she has a strong claim.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: ESTABLISHED DUTY CATEGORY · ROAD USER · ROBINSON · NO CAPARO REQUIRED · BREACH · CAUSATION · PHYSICAL INJURY

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: A local authority, Greenbrook Council, decides to close a youth centre that has historically reduced anti-social behaviour in the area. Following the closure, vandalism increases and a local shop, owned by Yusuf, suffers £40,000 of damage. Yusuf wishes to sue the Council in negligence for failing to continue operating the centre. Advise him.
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
This claim raises two overlapping difficulties:

1. Public body and policy decisions: Courts are very reluctant to impose liability on public authorities for the exercise of discretionary statutory functions — this crosses from operational negligence into policy-making, which is a function of elected government, not courts (X v Bedfordshire CC [1995] HL; Stovin v Wise [1996] HL).

2. Omission and third party acts: The claim is that the Council failed to act (omission) in a way that would have prevented third parties (vandals) from causing damage. This engages both the no-general-duty-for-omissions rule and the no-liability-for-third-party-acts rule.

3. Pure economic loss: The shop damage is property damage (recoverable in principle) but the closure caused it only through the intervening acts of third parties — the vandals. The causal chain is very attenuated.

4. No assumption of responsibility: The Council made no specific promise or undertaking to Yusuf or to the community.

Conclusion: Yusuf's claim is very unlikely to succeed. There is no duty of care in these circumstances — imposing one would fetter democratic decision-making and impose indeterminate liability on local authorities for budgetary choices.
Duty of Care — General Principles.summary_line: PUBLIC BODY · POLICY DECISION · NO DUTY · OMISSION · THIRD PARTY ACTS · STOVIN v WISE · X v BEDFORDSHIRE · INDETERMINATE LIABILITY

Duty of Care — General Principles.front: Going deeper: Is the incremental approach to duty of care in Robinson coherent, and does it entrench historical injustices within the law?
Duty of Care — General Principles.answer:
Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018]: Lord Reed confirmed that Caparo's three-stage test is not a universal formula — courts should first identify whether an established duty category applies. Only genuinely novel situations require the Caparo analysis. This prioritises certainty and predictability.

The entrenchment critique: Nolan and Robertson (2013) argued the incremental approach has a deeply conservative effect. By privileging established categories, it locks in historical decisions made at a time when the law was less developed and may have reflected social assumptions (about the police, public bodies, employers) that are no longer valid. To challenge a no-duty rule, a claimant must overcome strong precedent — not just argue justice on the current facts.

The police immunity problem: Hill v Chief Constable [1989] established a near-absolute immunity for police investigation and suppression of crime. This has been confirmed in Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales [2015] despite being widely criticised as leaving victims of police inaction without remedy. The ECtHR has found English law compatible with Article 6 ECHR in this context, but found breaches of the procedural limb of Article 2 (right to life) in cases of police failure to protect where they had specific knowledge of a threat (Osman v UK [1998]).

Caparo's role: The three-stage test was originally intended to restrain expansionary duty-of-care reasoning post-Anns. Robinson's effect is to marginalise it further — reserving it for genuinely novel cases while established categories govern everything else. McIvor (2018) argues this leaves Caparo as a residual discretionary tool rather than a principled foundation.

The Australian contrast: Australia rejected the incremental approach in Sullivan v Moody [2001] HCA and uses a 'salient features' analysis that is more openly policy-sensitive than English law. New Zealand uses a two-stage proximity test.

Conclusion: Robinson produces certainty at the cost of flexibility. Whether that trade-off is appropriate depends on one's view of whether courts or Parliament should reform established no-duty rules.

Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parties
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.front: What is the general rule on liability in negligence for a pure omission (failure to act)?
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.answer:
English law does not impose liability for a pure omission — there is no general duty to rescue a stranger (Stovin v Wise [1996] HL). Exceptions arise where:

1. The defendant assumed responsibility for the claimant (Hedley Byrne v Heller)
2. A pre-existing relationship gives rise to a duty to act (employer/employee, parent/child, doctor/patient)
3. The defendant created the dangerous situation that requires them to act
4. The defendant has control over a third party who causes harm (Home Office v Dorset Yacht [1970]).
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.summary_line: PURE OMISSIONS · NO GENERAL DUTY TO ACT · EXCEPTIONS · STOVIN v WISE · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · CREATED DANGER

Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parties
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.front: What is the general rule on liability in negligence for the acts of a third party, and what are the main exceptions?
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.answer:
There is generally no duty to prevent a third party from causing harm to another.

**Exceptions:**

1. The defendant assumed responsibility to control the third party (Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co [1970] — borstal officers liable for damage by escaping boys they controlled)
2. A special relationship between defendant and third party gives a degree of control
3. The defendant created a source of danger foreseeably exploited by the third party.

Foreseeability alone is insufficient — there must be proximity.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.summary_line: THIRD PARTY ACTS · NO GENERAL DUTY · EXCEPTIONS · CONTROL · DORSET YACHT · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · FORESEEABILITY INSUFFICIENT

Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parties
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.front: Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co Ltd [1970] HL
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.issue: Whether the Home Office and its borstal officers owed a duty of care to the owners of yachts damaged by escaping borstal trainees.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.rule: While there is generally no duty to prevent a third party causing harm, a duty may arise where the defendant has a special degree of control over a third party whose actions cause harm. The defendant must have assumed responsibility for the third party's conduct. Foreseeability of harm is necessary but not sufficient.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.application: Borstal officers took trainees on a work party to Brownsea Island. Three trainees absconded at night and boarded and damaged nearby yachts. The officers had failed to follow Home Office instructions about supervision. The HL held the Home Office owed a duty of care to the owners of the yachts because the officers had physical control over the trainees and damage was foreseeable.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.conclusion: The Home Office was liable. This case established an important exception to the no-liability rule for third party acts: where the defendant has control over the third party and the damage is foreseeable, a duty of care arises.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.summary_line: THIRD PARTY ACTS · CONTROL EXCEPTION · BORSTAL OFFICERS · FORESEEABLE DAMAGE · DUTY TO PROPERTY OWNERS · NO GENERAL DUTY TO PREVENT THIRD PARTY HARM

Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parties
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.front: Marcus is walking past a lake when he sees a child, Lily, struggling in the water. Marcus is a strong swimmer but chooses to walk on. Lily drowns. Lily's parents wish to bring a claim in negligence against Marcus. Advise them.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.answer:
The general rule: English law does not impose liability for a pure omission — there is no general duty to rescue a stranger (Stovin v Wise [1996] HL). Marcus's failure to enter the water is a pure omission.

**Exceptions:** A duty to act arises where (a) the defendant assumed responsibility for the claimant; (b) a special pre-existing relationship exists; (c) the defendant created the dangerous situation. None of these exceptions applies on the facts. Marcus had no prior relationship with Lily, had made no assumption of responsibility, and did not create the danger.

Conclusion: Marcus owes no duty of care to Lily. Her parents' claim will fail at the duty stage. English law has repeatedly declined to impose a general duty to rescue, even on those who could help at minimal cost to themselves.

**Note:** this is a morally uncomfortable outcome — reform in this area has been discussed but not implemented.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.summary_line: PURE OMISSION · NO DUTY TO RESCUE · STOVIN v WISE · NO EXCEPTIONS APPLY · NO ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY · POLICY

Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parties
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.front: The Southwick Mental Health Trust negligently discharges Carl, a patient with a known history of violence, without arranging adequate follow-up care. Three weeks later, Carl attacks and seriously injures his neighbour, Helen. Helen wishes to sue the Trust in negligence. Advise her.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.answer:
Liability for third party acts: There is generally no duty to prevent a third party causing harm. However, an exception arises where the defendant has a special degree of control over the third party (Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co [1970] HL).

Here, the Trust had Carl as an in-patient under its care and control. At the point of discharge, that control ceased. The key question is whether the Trust's assumption of responsibility for Carl's care, combined with its knowledge of his violent propensity, created a duty to third parties who might foreseeably be injured by him.

The courts have been cautious about imposing duties on health authorities to third parties injured by released patients (Palmer v Tees Health Authority [1999] CA — claimant unidentifiable from general public; no duty). Helen would need to show she was an identifiable, specific individual within a class at particular risk — a neighbour known to the Trust. This is stronger than Palmer but still uncertain.

Conclusion: The claim faces significant difficulty on duty. Proximity to a specific, identifiable victim assists Helen, but English courts have not clearly imposed liability in this type of case.

⚠️ Verify current case law development.
Duty of Care — Omissions and Third Parti.summary_line: THIRD PARTY ACTS · CONTROL EXCEPTION · DORSET YACHT · MENTAL HEALTH TRUST · PALMER v TEES · IDENTIFIABLE VICTIM · ⚠️ UNCERTAIN

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: What is pure economic loss and what is the general rule on its recoverability in negligence?
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.answer:
Pure economic loss is financial loss not consequential on physical injury to the claimant's person or property. The general rule is that pure economic loss is NOT recoverable in negligence for a negligent act (Spartan Steel & Alloys v Martin & Co [1973] CA). Reasons:

1. Indeterminacy of liability — the class of potential claimants is unlimited
2. Claimants can protect themselves by contract.

**Exception:** pure economic loss from a negligent misstatement may be recoverable where there is an assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance (Hedley Byrne & Co v Heller & Partners [1964] HL).
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · NOT RECOVERABLE · NEGLIGENT ACT · SPARTAN STEEL · HEDLEY BYRNE EXCEPTION · INDETERMINACY

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front:
Classify each loss as: (i) pure economic loss (generally not recoverable), (ii) consequential economic loss (recoverable), or (iii) pure economic loss recoverable under Hedley Byrne.

A. A surveyor gives a negligent valuation report; the buyer pays £30,000 over market value.
B. A driver negligently causes an accident; the claimant's car is destroyed and they lose two weeks' earnings while it is repaired.
C. A contractor negligently cuts a power cable; a nearby factory loses production revenue for eight hours.
D. A solicitor gives negligent advice on a property purchase; the client buys a property with a defective title.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.answer:
A. Pure economic loss recoverable under Hedley Byrne — iii. The surveyor voluntarily assumed responsibility for the valuation report, the buyer reasonably relied on it, and reliance was reasonable in the circumstances (Smith v Eric S Bush [1990]). A duty of care arises for the negligent misstatement.

B. Consequential economic loss — ii. The loss of earnings flows directly from physical damage to the claimant's property (the car). It is not pure economic loss — it is consequential on property damage and is recoverable.

C. Pure economic loss (not recoverable) — i. The factory suffered no physical injury to its person or property. The revenue loss is pure financial loss caused by a negligent act, not a statement. Not recoverable under general negligence principles (Spartan Steel & Alloys v Martin [1973]).

D. Pure economic loss recoverable under Hedley Byrne — iii. A solicitor advising a client assumes responsibility for that advice; the client reasonably relies on it. The financial loss from the defective title is pure economic loss but falls within the Hedley Byrne exception.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: CLASSIFY LOSS · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · CONSEQUENTIAL ECONOMIC LOSS · HEDLEY BYRNE EXCEPTION · SPARTAN STEEL · SMITH v ERIC BUSH

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: Spartan Steel & Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] CA
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.issue: Whether a contractor who negligently damaged a power cable owed a duty of care to a factory that suffered loss of production during the resulting power cut.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.rule: Pure economic loss — financial loss not consequential on physical damage to the claimant's person or property — is generally irrecoverable in negligence for a negligent act. Consequential economic loss (flowing from property damage) is recoverable; pure economic loss (financial loss alone, unconnected to physical damage) is not.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.application: The defendant contractor cut an electricity cable while digging, causing a power cut. The claimant factory suffered: (a) physical damage to a melt in the furnace (recoverable); (b) loss of profit on that melt (recoverable as consequential); (c) loss of profit on further melts during the power cut — this was pure economic loss, as the factory itself was undamaged. The CA denied recovery for (c).
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.conclusion: Pure economic loss from the power cut was not recoverable. Lord Denning MR gave the policy reasons: loss of production during power cuts is a widespread risk; imposing liability would be disproportionate and indeterminate. The case is the leading authority on the no-recovery rule for pure economic loss from negligent acts.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · NOT RECOVERABLE · NEGLIGENT ACT · POWER CUT · CONSEQUENTIAL LOSS RECOVERABLE · INDETERMINACY POLICY · LORD DENNING

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] HL
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.issue: Whether a local authority that negligently approved defective building plans owed a duty of care to a subsequent purchaser of the building who suffered pure economic loss when the defect was discovered.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.rule: A defective building that has not yet caused physical injury to person or property represents a pure economic loss — the diminution in value of the building. Pure economic loss caused by a negligent act (not statement) is generally not recoverable. The HL overruled Anns v Merton LBC [1978], which had permitted such claims, because it was inconsistent with the rule against recovery of pure economic loss.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.application: Mr Murphy bought a house that had been built on a cracked concrete raft foundation. The defect had been approved by the local authority inspector. The crack became apparent and rendered the house unsafe. Mr Murphy sold the house at a loss. He had not suffered personal injury or physical damage to other property — only the loss on the sale of the defective house itself.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.conclusion: No duty of care. The HL (unanimously) overruled Anns. The loss was pure economic loss: damage to the defective property itself. This is not recoverable in negligence. Murphy is the leading authority confirming that defects in buildings causing diminution in value only do not give rise to liability in negligence.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: MURPHY v BRENTWOOD · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · DEFECTIVE BUILDING · OVERRULED ANNS · DIMINUTION IN VALUE · NOT RECOVERABLE · NO PHYSICAL INJURY · NEGLIGENT ACT

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: An engineer negligently damages a gas main while digging. The gas supply to a nearby restaurant, La Maison, is cut off for 12 hours. La Maison suffers no physical damage to its premises or equipment but loses an evening's takings of £4,000. Advise La Maison.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.answer:
Pure economic loss — negligent act: La Maison's loss is purely financial. No physical damage has been caused to La Maison's property or to the person of anyone at La Maison. The loss of earnings flows entirely from the interruption to services, not from any physical damage.

The general rule: Pure economic loss caused by a negligent act is not recoverable in negligence (Spartan Steel & Alloys v Martin & Co [1973] CA). In Spartan Steel, loss of profit during a power cut caused by negligent cable damage was irrecoverable as pure economic loss. The position of La Maison is directly analogous — loss of trade during a gas disruption is pure economic loss.

No Hedley Byrne exception: There is no negligent statement — only a negligent act. The Hedley Byrne exception does not apply.

Conclusion: La Maison cannot recover its £4,000 loss in negligence. The engineer owes no duty of care for this type of loss. La Maison may have a contractual claim against the gas supplier depending on its supply contract.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · NEGLIGENT ACT · NOT RECOVERABLE · SPARTAN STEEL · GAS MAIN · LOSS OF TRADE · NO PHYSICAL DAMAGE · NO HEDLEY BYRNE

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: A structural engineer, Paulo, negligently certifies that a commercial building is sound. InvestCo purchases the building relying on the certificate. The defects later emerge; the building requires £200,000 of remedial work. InvestCo had no contract with Paulo — it was the seller who engaged him. Advise InvestCo.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.answer:
Negligent misstatement and assumption of responsibility: InvestCo's loss is the cost of remedial works — pure economic loss (the building itself is defective; no personal injury or damage to other property). Murphy v Brentwood DC [1991] HL confirms that defects in a building causing diminution in value only are pure economic loss, irrecoverable under a general negligence duty.

However, InvestCo can argue a Hedley Byrne duty:

1. Paulo issued a structural certificate — a statement of professional opinion;

2. Did Paulo assume responsibility to InvestCo? The certificate was prepared for the seller, not InvestCo.

This is a Caparo-type problem — was the statement prepared for the purpose of being relied upon by InvestCo in particular?

If Paulo knew the certificate would be shown to a buyer to support a sale — as is common practice — assumption of responsibility is arguable (cf. Smith v Eric S Bush [1990]: surveyor's report for mortgage company was relied upon by buyer; duty owed). The commercial scale of this transaction (cf. a domestic property in Smith v Bush) may make the courts more cautious.

Conclusion: InvestCo's best argument is Hedley Byrne, arguing Paulo assumed responsibility to any buyer relying on the certificate for the purpose of the sale. Success depends on whether Paulo knew the certificate would be so used.

⚠️ Outcome uncertain in commercial context.
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.summary_line: PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · MURPHY v BRENTWOOD · DEFECTIVE BUILDING · HEDLEY BYRNE POSSIBLE · ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY · PURPOSE · SMITH v BUSH · ⚠️ COMMERCIAL

Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.subtopic: Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.front: Going deeper: Is the rule against recovery of pure economic loss from negligent acts coherent, and how does the Hedley Byrne exception sit within it?
Duty of Care — Pure Economic Loss.answer:
The no-recovery rule (Spartan Steel [1973]; Murphy v Brentwood [1991]) has been justified on three grounds:

1. Floodgates — unlimited liability to an unlimited class;

2. Economic efficiency — losses should lie where they fall unless there is good reason to transfer them;

3. Contractual allocation — parties should allocate economic risk by contract.

Academic critique: Bussani and Palmer (eds, Pure Economic Loss in Europe, 2003) demonstrate that most civil law systems recover pure economic loss without the floodgates feared by English courts. Cane (Tort Law and Economic Interests, 1996) argues the distinction between physical damage and economic loss is doctrinally unstable — physical damage to property is itself just economic loss in another form.

The Hedley Byrne exception: The assumption of responsibility principle (Hedley Byrne [1964]; Henderson v Merrett [1995]) has expanded considerably. It is no longer confined to statements — it covers professional services, conveyancing, and (arguably) any relationship where one party undertakes responsibility for another's economic interests. Stevens (Torts and Rights, 2007) argues the exception has swallowed much of the rule — the courts have used it to reach just outcomes while nominally preserving the no-recovery principle.

Murphy v Brentwood tension: The decision to overrule Anns v Merton [1978] was driven by concerns about indeterminate liability to successive purchasers of defective property. Yet the result — that a defective building causing no injury to anyone is irrecoverable — sits uneasily with the consumer protection aims of tort law. New Zealand declined to follow Murphy in Invercargill City Council v Hamlin [1996].

Current direction: The SC has shown no appetite for wholesale reform. The Hedley Byrne exception continues to expand incrementally, effectively creating a 'back door' to recovery of economic loss in appropriate relationships.

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: What are the requirements for a duty of care in negligent misstatement causing pure economic loss (Hedley Byrne)?
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.answer:
Under Hedley Byrne & Co v Heller & Partners [1964] HL, a duty of care arises for a negligent misstatement where:

1. The defendant voluntarily assumed responsibility for the statement or advice
2. The claimant reasonably relied on that statement
3. It was reasonable in the circumstances for the claimant to rely on it (a 'special relationship').

A disclaimer can negate the assumption of responsibility (as in Hedley Byrne itself). The principle has been extended to services: Henderson v Merrett Syndicates [1995] HL. Caparo Industries v Dickman [1990]: auditors owed no duty to investors relying on statutory accounts.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: HEDLEY BYRNE · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · REASONABLE RELIANCE · SPECIAL RELATIONSHIP · DISCLAIMER · HENDERSON v MERRETT

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: In what circumstances will an assumption of responsibility be negated, and what is the effect of a disclaimer?
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.answer:
An assumption of responsibility can be negated by:

1. Express disclaimer — the defendant expressly excludes responsibility (Hedley Byrne v Heller — bank's disclaimer prevented liability)
2. Implied disclaimer — the context makes clear no assumption was intended
3. Unreasonable reliance by the claimant. A disclaimer is only effective if it satisfies the reasonableness test under UCTA 1977 s.2
2. where business liability for pure economic loss is being excluded (Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] HL — surveyors' disclaimer unreasonable on ordinary house purchase).

In consumer contexts, CRA 2015 may also render disclaimers ineffective.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY NEGATED · DISCLAIMER · HEDLEY BYRNE · UCTA 1977 S.2(2) · SMITH v ERIC BUSH · REASONABLENESS

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] HL
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.issue: Whether a duty of care could arise in negligence for a pure economic loss caused by a negligent misstatement, where there was no contract between the parties.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.rule:
A duty of care in respect of a negligent misstatement causing pure economic loss arises where:

1. the defendant voluntarily assumed responsibility for the statement or advice
2. the claimant reasonably relied on that statement
3. it was reasonable in the circumstances for the claimant to rely on it (a 'special relationship').

A clear disclaimer can negate the assumption of responsibility.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.application: Hedley Byrne asked Heller (a bank) for a credit reference on a client. Heller gave a favourable reference but headed it 'without responsibility'. Hedley Byrne extended credit in reliance; the client defaulted and Hedley Byrne lost money. The HL held that the special relationship giving rise to a duty existed on these facts — but liability was excluded by the disclaimer.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.conclusion: Obiter: a duty of care in negligent misstatement can arise via a 'special relationship' involving assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance. On the facts, the disclaimer prevented liability. This decision created the Hedley Byrne exception to the rule that pure economic loss is irrecoverable in negligence.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: NEGLIGENT MISSTATEMENT · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · REASONABLE RELIANCE · SPECIAL RELATIONSHIP · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · DISCLAIMER DEFEATS · OBITER DUTY

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: Henderson v Merrett Syndicates Ltd [1995] HL
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.issue: Whether Lloyd's managing agents owed a duty of care in tort to Names (investors) for whom they managed underwriting syndicates, even where there was an underlying contract.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.rule: The Hedley Byrne principle extends beyond statements to the negligent performance of services. Where one party assumes responsibility for the exercise of a skill for the benefit of another, and the other relies on that skill, a duty of care in tort exists alongside (and not displaced by) any contractual duty.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.application: Managing agents at Lloyd's managed underwriting on behalf of Names. Many Names were not in direct contract with the agents. The agents incurred huge losses through negligent underwriting. The HL held the Hedley Byrne assumption of responsibility principle applied — the agents had undertaken to exercise skill on behalf of the Names, who relied on them.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.conclusion: The managing agents owed a duty of care in tort to all Names, including those with whom they had no direct contract. Concurrent liability in contract and tort is possible. Henderson confirmed assumption of responsibility as the central concept in pure economic loss claims.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: HEDLEY BYRNE EXTENDED TO SERVICES · ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY · CONCURRENT LIABILITY · LLOYD'S · NO CONTRACT REQUIRED · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] HL
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.issue: Whether a surveyor who provided a negligent valuation report for mortgage purposes owed a duty of care to the house purchaser, and whether a disclaimer of liability was effective.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.rule: A surveyor who values a property for mortgage purposes owes a duty of care to the purchaser who relies on the report, even though the surveyor is engaged by and reports to the lender. The assumption of responsibility extends to the purchaser because it is foreseeable that the purchaser will rely on the report rather than commission their own survey. A disclaimer is subject to the reasonableness test under UCTA 1977 s.2(2) — on an ordinary domestic property purchase, a disclaimer is unreasonable and ineffective.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.application: Mr Smith bought a house relying on a mortgage valuation report carried out by Eric S Bush for the building society. The report negligently failed to note that chimney breasts had been removed without support; part of the house collapsed. The surveyors relied on a disclaimer in the report excluding liability to the purchaser.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.conclusion: The surveyors were liable. A duty was owed to the purchaser; the disclaimer was unreasonable under UCTA 1977 s.2(2) because it would have been oppressive to require purchasers of modest homes to pay for their own survey. The case is a leading application of Hedley Byrne and UCTA in the conveyancing context.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: SMITH v ERIC BUSH · SURVEYOR · DUTY TO PURCHASER · HEDLEY BYRNE · DISCLAIMER UNREASONABLE · UCTA 1977 S.2(2) · MORTGAGE VALUATION · DOMESTIC PROPERTY

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: Fiona, an accountant, is asked by her client GreenLeaf Ltd to prepare management accounts. She knows the accounts will be shown to a potential investor, TechVentures plc, to support a funding round. Fiona makes negligent errors; TechVentures invests £500,000 in reliance on the accounts and loses it all when GreenLeaf fails. Advise TechVentures.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.answer:
Negligent misstatement (Hedley Byrne & Co v Heller [1964] HL): A duty of care for pure economic loss from a statement arises where

1. the defendant assumed responsibility for the statement
2. the claimant reasonably relied on it
3. reliance was reasonable in the circumstances.

Here:
1. Fiona knew the accounts would be shown to TechVentures specifically for the purpose of the investment decision — this is key. Assumption of responsibility is strongly arguable where the defendant knows the identity of the recipient and the purpose (Caparo v Dickman [1990] — auditors owed no duty to investors relying on statutory accounts NOT prepared for that purpose; the distinction is that here Fiona knew the accounts were prepared for TechVentures' specific use)
2. TechVentures relied on the accounts — this is clear
3. Reliance was reasonable — TechVentures was given the accounts for investment purposes.

Conclusion: A duty of care is likely owed by Fiona to TechVentures. The facts are closer to Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] than Caparo — Fiona knew the purpose and the recipient. TechVentures has a strong Hedley Byrne claim.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: HEDLEY BYRNE · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · KNOWN PURPOSE · KNOWN RECIPIENT · DISTINGUISHES CAPARO · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS · ACCOUNTANT

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: At a dinner party, Hugo — a solicitor — is asked by his friend Raj about the planning rules for converting a garage into a home office. Hugo gives informal advice, saying planning permission is not needed. Raj spends £25,000 on the conversion; the council orders him to reinstate the garage as the works did require consent. Raj wants to sue Hugo. Advise Raj.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.answer:
Hedley Byrne — was there an assumption of responsibility? The key question is whether, in giving advice at a social occasion, Hugo assumed responsibility to Raj.

Courts examine the context carefully. In Chaudhry v Prabhakar [1988] CA, informal advice given by a friend was held sufficient to found a duty. However, the more informal and social the setting, the less likely an assumption of responsibility will be found.

Factors against Raj: (a) advice was given at a dinner party, not in a professional context; (b) there was no retainer or fee; (c) Hugo may not have known Raj would rely on the advice to spend £25,000.

Factors for Raj: (a) Hugo is a solicitor — his expertise was the reason Raj asked; (b) Hugo gave a confident answer without qualification; (c) a professional who answers questions in their area of expertise even informally may still be taken to have assumed responsibility.

Conclusion: This is a borderline case. Raj's strongest argument is that Hugo gave confident professional advice without disclaimer. Hugo's strongest counter is the social context. Adding a disclaimer at the time (e.g., 'seek formal advice') would have negated responsibility.

⚠️ Outcome uncertain — advise Raj the claim is arguable but not straightforward.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: HEDLEY BYRNE · SOCIAL OCCASION · ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY · CHAUDHRY v PRABHAKAR · NO DISCLAIMER · PROFESSIONAL GIVING ADVICE · ⚠️ BORDERLINE

Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.front: Crestline Bank provides a credit reference to Omega Ltd about a third party, Dex Supplies. The reference is headed 'without responsibility'. In reliance on the reference, Omega extends £80,000 of credit to Dex Supplies, which then becomes insolvent. Omega sues Crestline Bank. Advise Crestline Bank.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.answer:
Hedley Byrne — disclaimer: These facts mirror Hedley Byrne & Co v Heller & Partners [1964] HL precisely. In Hedley Byrne, the bank gave a favourable credit reference 'without responsibility'. The HL held a special relationship existed giving rise to a duty of care in principle — but the disclaimer negated the assumption of responsibility, preventing liability.

Here, Crestline Bank's 'without responsibility' heading is an express disclaimer. This negates the assumption of responsibility and is likely effective, provided:

1. The disclaimer was brought to Omega's attention before reliance — it was on the reference itself, so yes;

2. The disclaimer is not rendered ineffective by UCTA 1977 s.2
2. (reasonableness test for business-to-business exclusion of liability for economic loss).

In a commercial context between two businesses, a disclaimer on a credit reference is likely to be reasonable — unlike Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] (domestic consumer context).

Conclusion: Crestline Bank has a strong defence. The disclaimer negates the assumption of responsibility. The UCTA reasonableness test is likely satisfied in a commercial context. Omega's claim should fail.
Duty of Care — Negligent Misstatement.summary_line: HEDLEY BYRNE · DISCLAIMER · NEGATES ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY · UCTA 1977 S.2(2) · COMMERCIAL CONTEXT · REASONABLE · MIRRORS HEDLEY BYRNE FACTS

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: What is the distinction between primary and secondary victims in claims for psychiatric harm in negligence?
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
A primary victim is someone within the range of foreseeable physical injury who also suffers psychiatric harm. A secondary victim is outside the zone of physical danger and suffers psychiatric harm by witnessing injury to another. Secondary victims must satisfy the Alcock control mechanisms (Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1992] HL):

1. A close tie of love and affection with the primary victim
2. Proximity in time and space to the accident or its immediate aftermath
3. Direct perception through their own unaided senses — not via TV or radio.

⚠️ The rule that a primary victim need only show that personal injury of some kind was foreseeable (Page v Smith [1996]) was overruled by the Supreme Court in Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1. The precise test for primary victims is now resettled; verify the current SRA SQE1 syllabus position.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: PRIMARY VICTIM · SECONDARY VICTIM · ALCOCK · THREE REQUIREMENTS · ⚠️ PAGE v SMITH OVERRULED BY PAUL [2024] · UNAIDED SENSES

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: What did Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1992] decide, and what control mechanisms did it establish?
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1992] HL: relatives and friends of Hillsborough victims claimed for psychiatric harm suffered through watching events on TV or being told by third parties. All claims were dismissed. The House of Lords established three control mechanisms for secondary victims:

1. Close tie of love and affection — presumed for spouse and parent/child; must be proved for others
2. Proximity to the accident or its immediate aftermath
3. Perception through their own unaided senses — live TV broadcast was insufficient.

⚠️ The Supreme Court in Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1 overruled Page v Smith but confirmed that the Alcock control mechanisms remain good law. Secondary victim claims continue to require all three conditions.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: ALCOCK · SECONDARY VICTIMS · HILLSBOROUGH · CLOSE TIE · PROXIMITY · UNAIDED SENSES · ALCOCK UPHELD BY PAUL [2024]

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: What did Page v Smith [1996] decide about primary victims and psychiatric harm, and what is its current status?
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Page v Smith [1996] HL: the claimant was in a minor road accident and suffered no physical injury, but his pre-existing chronic fatigue syndrome (ME) became permanent. The House of Lords held: for a primary victim, it was sufficient that some personal injury (physical or psychiatric) was reasonably foreseeable — psychiatric injury itself need not be foreseeable.

⚠️ Page v Smith was OVERRULED by the UK Supreme Court in Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1. The Supreme Court held Page was wrongly decided. The correct position is now that a claimant seeking to recover for psychiatric harm must show that psychiatric harm (or recognised mental illness) was itself a foreseeable consequence of the defendant's breach. Verify against current SRA SQE1 guidance before relying on Page v Smith in revision.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: PAGE v SMITH · ⚠️ OVERRULED BY PAUL v ROYAL WOLVERHAMPTON [2024] UKSC 1 · PSYCHIATRIC HARM MUST ITSELF BE FORESEEABLE

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front:
Apply the secondary victim checklist to the following scenario:

Maria witnesses a serious road accident caused by the defendant's negligent driving. The accident kills her close friend of 20 years. Maria was standing on the pavement 15 metres away and saw the collision happen directly. She develops severe PTSD. Advise whether Maria can recover as a secondary victim. Apply each Alcock control mechanism in turn.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Secondary victims must satisfy the three Alcock control mechanisms (Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1992] HL).

**Note:** Page v Smith [1996] has been overruled by Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1 — the primary/secondary victim distinction for psychiatric harm must now be applied with care.

1. Close tie of love and affection with the primary victim: A close friendship of 20 years is not within the presumed categories (spouse, parent/child). Maria must prove the closeness of the relationship. On these facts, a 20-year close friendship is likely sufficient if she can adduce evidence — but it is not automatic.

2. Proximity in time and space to the accident or its immediate aftermath: Maria was present at the scene and witnessed the collision directly. This condition is satisfied — she was there at the time.

3. Perception through her own unaided senses: Maria saw the accident with her own eyes from 15 metres away. This condition is satisfied — she did not learn of events through television, radio, or a third party.

Conclusion: Maria satisfies conditions 2 and 3. Condition 1 depends on proof of the tie of love and affection. If she can establish this, she has a strong claim. She must also prove a recognised psychiatric illness (PTSD qualifies).
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: SECONDARY VICTIM CHECKLIST · ALCOCK · CLOSE TIE · PROXIMITY · UNAIDED SENSES · PTSD RECOGNISED · PAUL [2024] · FRIENDSHIP NOT PRESUMED

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: Alcock v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1992] HL
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.issue: Whether relatives and friends of victims of the Hillsborough disaster, who suffered psychiatric illness after witnessing events on live television or being told by third parties, could recover as secondary victims.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.rule:
A secondary victim (outside the zone of physical danger) who suffers psychiatric illness must satisfy three control mechanisms:

1. a close tie of love and affection with the primary victim (presumed for spouse and parent/child; must be proved for others)
2. proximity in time and space to the accident or its immediate aftermath
3. direct perception through their own unaided senses — not via television or radio.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.application: 16 claimants sought damages for psychiatric illness suffered as a result of the Hillsborough disaster. Some watched on live TV; some were told by others. None had been in physical danger. Many had brothers, brothers-in-law, or friends among the victims — relationships not presumed to carry close ties. The HL dismissed all claims.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.conclusion: All claims failed. The Alcock criteria established the control mechanisms for secondary victim claims. Watching via live TV did not satisfy the unaided senses requirement. Relationships beyond the presumed category required individual proof of close ties. These mechanisms remain good law after Paul [2024].
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: SECONDARY VICTIMS · THREE CONTROL MECHANISMS · CLOSE TIE · PROXIMITY · UNAIDED SENSES · TV INSUFFICIENT · HILLSBOROUGH · PAUL [2024] RETAINED ALCOCK

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1999] HL
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.issue: Whether police officers who were present at the Hillsborough disaster and suffered psychiatric illness as a result of what they witnessed could recover as primary victims, or were subject to the Alcock secondary victim control mechanisms.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.rule: A professional rescuer who is not within the zone of foreseeable physical injury does not automatically become a primary victim. They are treated as secondary victims and must satisfy the Alcock control mechanisms. The mere fact of being a rescuer does not exempt a claimant from the secondary victim rules if they were not themselves in physical danger.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.application: Police officers at Hillsborough claimed PTSD from what they had witnessed and done. The HL held they had not been in physical danger themselves and could not claim as primary victims. As secondary victims, they failed Alcock — they had no close tie of love and affection with the football supporters who were killed or injured.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.conclusion: The police officers' claims failed. The HL rejected the argument that professional rescuers should be treated as primary victims regardless of physical danger. Alcock criteria apply. Distinguishable from Chadwick v BRB [1967], where the rescuer was in physical danger.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: PROFESSIONAL RESCUERS · NOT PRIMARY VICTIMS · SECONDARY VICTIM CRITERIA · ALCOCK APPLIES · NO CLOSE TIE · HILLSBOROUGH · DISTINGUISHED FROM CHADWICK

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.issue: Whether the rule in Page v Smith [1996] — that for a primary victim it is sufficient that personal injury of any kind was foreseeable — remained good law.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.rule: Page v Smith [1996] is overruled. The correct approach for both primary and secondary victims is that the defendant must have breached a duty of care owed to the claimant to protect them from the kind of harm suffered. For psychiatric injury claims, it is not sufficient that the defendant should have foreseen personal injury of any kind — psychiatric injury must itself be a foreseeable consequence of the breach. The Alcock secondary victim control mechanisms are retained.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.application: The claimants suffered psychiatric illness after witnessing family members die from heart attacks allegedly caused by earlier NHS negligence. The SC considered whether negligence in the earlier medical treatment gave rise to a duty to protect against the psychiatric harm suffered by witnessing the death.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.conclusion:
Page v Smith overruled. The SC held that foreseeability of personal injury of any kind is not sufficient to establish a duty in respect of psychiatric harm. The Alcock secondary victim control mechanisms are retained.

Accident vs medical crisis (key SQE distinction): Paul applies specifically where the 'shocking event' is the downstream consequence of a prior clinical omission — not a discrete external accident. Where a secondary victim witnesses a sudden external accident caused by negligence (e.g. a road collision), the Alcock criteria continue to govern. Where they witness a medical crisis resulting from a prior failure to diagnose or treat, Paul bars the secondary victim claim entirely, regardless of whether the Alcock criteria are met.

⚠️ SQE1 candidates should note: the SRA frequently presents a time lag between the negligent act and the shocking event — this signals the Paul medical omission scenario, not an Alcock accident scenario.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: ⚠️ PAGE v SMITH OVERRULED · PSYCHIATRIC INJURY MUST BE FORESEEABLE · ALCOCK RETAINED · ACCIDENT vs MEDICAL CRISIS · LAG = PAUL SCENARIO · SECONDARY VICTIMS UNCHANGED FOR ACCIDENTS

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: Ned is a passenger in a car driven negligently by his friend Otto. The car crashes; Ned escapes physical injury but narrowly avoids being crushed and suffers severe PTSD. Advise Ned on whether he can recover for his psychiatric injury.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Primary or secondary victim? Ned was in the car that crashed. He was therefore within the zone of foreseeable physical injury — he narrowly escaped being crushed. This makes him a primary victim.

Primary victim test post-Paul [2024]: Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1 overruled Page v Smith [1996]. The question now is whether Otto owed Ned a duty to protect him from psychiatric injury in these circumstances. Where a primary victim is within the zone of physical danger and physical injury was foreseeable, a duty to protect against psychiatric harm is also likely to be established as part of the general duty of care owed by a driver to a passenger.

PTSD is a recognised psychiatric illness — it is not mere grief or distress.

Conclusion: Ned is a primary victim within the zone of physical danger. Otto owed him a duty of care as a passenger; that duty extends to foreseeable psychiatric harm. Ned has a strong claim. The Alcock secondary victim criteria do not apply to him.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: PRIMARY VICTIM · ZONE OF PHYSICAL DANGER · PTSD RECOGNISED · PAUL [2024] · DUTY OF CARE · DRIVER TO PASSENGER · ALCOCK NOT APPLICABLE

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: Sandra is at home when she receives a phone call telling her that her husband has been seriously injured in a factory explosion caused by her employer's negligence. She rushes to the hospital and sees him in intensive care, disfigured. She develops clinical depression. Advise Sandra.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Secondary victim: Sandra was not at the scene and was not in physical danger. She is a secondary victim.

Alcock control mechanisms:

1. Close tie of love and affection — Sandra is the injured man's wife. This is within the presumed category (spouse); she satisfies this criterion.

2. Proximity in time and space — Sandra was not at the scene. She arrived at the hospital after the event. The 'immediate aftermath' doctrine may assist her: in McLoughlin v O'Brian [1983] HL, the claimant who saw her injured family at hospital shortly after the accident satisfied proximity. Sandra's situation is similar — she saw her husband in intensive care shortly after being called. This condition is likely satisfied.

3. Direct perception through unaided senses — Sandra did not perceive the accident itself; she was told by telephone.

However, she directly perceived the immediate aftermath (husband in intensive care) through her own senses at hospital. Alcock requires direct perception of the accident or its immediate aftermath. This criterion is likely met.

Clinical depression is a recognised psychiatric illness.

Conclusion: Sandra has a reasonable claim as a secondary victim. All three Alcock criteria appear satisfied, drawing on McLoughlin v O'Brian [1983]. The weakest point is the gap between the accident and hospital arrival, but on McLoughlin this is likely within the 'immediate aftermath'.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: SECONDARY VICTIM · ALCOCK · WIFE = PRESUMED CLOSE TIE · IMMEDIATE AFTERMATH · McLOUGHLIN v O'BRIAN · HOSPITAL VISIT · DIRECT PERCEPTION

Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.front: PC Grant attends the scene of a serious road accident caused by the defendant's negligence. He assists in extracting victims and witnesses severe injuries. He develops PTSD. He was not himself in physical danger. Advise PC Grant.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.answer:
Professional rescuer: PC Grant is a police officer attending in his professional capacity. He was not within the zone of physical danger.

White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police [1999] HL: The HL held that professional rescuers who are not themselves in physical danger are not treated as primary victims. They must satisfy the Alcock secondary victim criteria.

Alcock applied to PC Grant:
(1) Close tie of love and affection — PC Grant has no close tie with the accident victims. This criterion is NOT satisfied. The victims are strangers to him.

Conclusion: PC Grant's claim will fail. He cannot satisfy the Alcock secondary victim criteria because he has no close tie with any victim. White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire Police directly governs this situation.

Distinguishing Chadwick v BRB [1967]: In Chadwick, the rescuer was in physical danger during the rescue (he entered a wrecked train). If PC Grant had been in physical danger (e.g., risk of secondary collision), he might qualify as a primary victim. On the facts, he was not.

**Note:** If PC Grant had suffered physical injury in addition to PTSD, the analysis would differ — the physical injury claim would succeed and PTSD would follow as consequential harm.
Duty of Care — Psychiatric Harm.summary_line: PROFESSIONAL RESCUER · WHITE v CHIEF CONSTABLE · NOT PRIMARY VICTIM · ALCOCK APPLIES · NO CLOSE TIE · CHADWICK DISTINGUISHED · PTSD · FAILS ALCOCK

Duty of Care — Public Bodies.front: Do the police owe a duty of care to individual members of the public in the investigation or prevention of crime?
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.answer:
No, as a general rule. In Hill v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [1989] HL (Yorkshire Ripper case), the House of Lords held that police owe no duty of care to individual members of the public for the investigation or suppression of crime. Reasons:

1. Insufficient proximity between police and the public at large
2. Public policy — liability would lead to defensive policing.

Confirmed in Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] SC.

**Exception:** where police have assumed responsibility to a specific individual, a duty may arise (Swinney v Chief Constable of Northumbria [1997] — confidential informer).
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.summary_line: POLICE · NO GENERAL DUTY · HILL v WEST YORKSHIRE · MICHAEL v SOUTH WALES · ASSUMPTION OF RESPONSIBILITY EXCEPTION · SWINNEY

Duty of Care — Public Bodies.front: Hill v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [1989] HL
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.issue: Whether the police owed a duty of care to a victim of the Yorkshire Ripper murderer, in respect of their failure to apprehend him before she was killed.
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.rule: As a general rule, the police owe no duty of care to members of the public in the investigation or suppression of crime. Reasons: insufficient proximity between police and the public at large; public policy — imposing liability would lead to defensive, inefficient policing and divert resources to litigation.
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.application: The mother of Jacqueline Hill — the Ripper's final victim — sued the West Yorkshire Police for failing to apprehend Sutcliffe despite having had opportunities to do so. The HL struck out the claim on the basis that no duty of care was owed by police to individual members of the public who might become crime victims.
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.conclusion: No duty of care. Insufficient proximity between the police and any particular victim of a criminal at large. Public policy reinforced the no-duty rule. Hill remains the leading authority, confirmed in Michael v Chief Constable of South Wales Police [2015] SC.
Duty of Care — Public Bodies.summary_line: POLICE · NO GENERAL DUTY · INVESTIGATION OF CRIME · INSUFFICIENT PROXIMITY · PUBLIC POLICY · YORKSHIRE RIPPER · CONFIRMED IN MICHAEL [2015]

Duty of Care — Employers.front: What non-delegable duties does an employer owe to employees at common law?
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
An employer owes employees the following non-delegable duties (Wilsons & Clyde Coal Co v English [1938] HL):

1. To provide a safe place of work
2. To provide a safe system of work
3. To provide competent fellow employees
4. To provide safe, adequate plant and equipment (supplemented by the Employer's Liability (Defective Equipment) Act 1969).

These duties cannot be discharged by delegating them to a competent third party — the employer remains personally liable if the system or place of work is unsafe.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: EMPLOYER NON-DELEGABLE DUTIES · SAFE PLACE · SAFE SYSTEM · COMPETENT COLLEAGUES · EQUIPMENT · WILSONS & CLYDE COAL · ELDA 1969

Duty of Care — Employers.front: What is the employer's non-delegable duty of care and what four components does it comprise?
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
An employer owes a **personal, non-delegable duty** of care to each employee to take reasonable care for their safety at work. The duty cannot be discharged by delegating it to an independent contractor or fellow employee — if those persons are negligent, the employer remains liable.

**The four components (Wilsons and Clyde Coal Co v English [1938], HL):**
1. **Safe place of work** — safe premises and means of access.
2. **Safe system of work** — proper organisation, supervision, instructions, and working practices.
3. **Safe equipment** — provision and maintenance of safe tools and plant.
4. **Safe and competent fellow employees** — reasonable care in selection, retention, and supervision of staff.

**Non-delegable:** If the employer hires an independent contractor to fulfil one of these duties (e.g. maintain equipment) and the contractor is negligent, the employer is still liable — the duty cannot be outsourced away (*McDermid v Nash Dredging [1987]*).

**Standard:** Reasonable care in all the circumstances — this is a negligence standard, not strict liability. The employer must do what a reasonable employer would do to address a known or foreseeable risk.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: EMPLOYER'S DUTY · NON-DELEGABLE · WILSONS AND CLYDE COAL · SAFE PLACE · SAFE SYSTEM · SAFE EQUIPMENT · COMPETENT STAFF · REASONABLE CARE

Duty of Care — Employers.front: How does the employer's duty to provide a safe place of work apply where an employee has a known vulnerability? Refer to Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951].
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
The employer's duty to provide a safe place of work (and safe conditions generally) requires the employer to take reasonable precautions against **foreseeable risks**. Where an employee has a known vulnerability, the reasonable precautions required may be higher than for an average employee.

**Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951] (HL):**
- Paris was employed in a garage and was blind in one eye — the employer knew this.
- He was given no goggles. A chip of metal flew into his good eye and he was rendered completely blind.
- The issue: were goggles required? The risk of a fragment entering one eye was small; standard practice at the time was not to provide goggles for this work.
- **Held:** The employer was negligent. Even if goggles were not required for a sighted employee (the risk to them was minor, causing partial sight loss), the **catastrophic consequence** for Paris — total blindness — was foreseeable and the precaution (providing goggles) was minimal. The greater the potential harm, the greater the care required.

**Principle from Paris:** Severity of potential harm to a *particular* employee is a relevant factor in assessing what precautions a reasonable employer should take. Knowledge of vulnerability elevates the standard of care.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: SAFE PLACE OF WORK · PARIS V STEPNEY · KNOWN VULNERABILITY · SEVERITY OF HARM · GOGGLES · FORESEEABLE CATASTROPHIC HARM · ELEVATED STANDARD

Duty of Care — Employers.front: What does the employer's duty to provide safe equipment require, and how did Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] define the limits of that duty?
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
The employer's duty to provide **safe equipment** requires taking reasonable care to select, supply, and maintain safe tools, machinery, and plant, and to warn employees of known defects.

**Latimer v AEC Ltd [1953] (HL):**
- A factory was flooded by heavy rain; oil and water mixed on the floor, making it slippery.
- The employer put down sawdust on most of the floor but could not cover all areas.
- Latimer slipped on an uncovered area and was injured.
- He argued the employer should have closed the factory until the floor was safe.
- **Held:** The employer was not negligent. The risk (a slippery floor) was a known hazard but the employer had taken reasonable steps (sawdust). The cost and disruption of closing the factory was disproportionate to the risk — a reasonable employer would not have closed the factory.

**Principle from Latimer:** The employer must take **reasonable** precautions proportionate to the risk. Where the risk is foreseeable but limited, and the employer has taken reasonable steps to reduce it, the duty does not require extreme measures (such as shutting down operations). This reflects the general breach of duty calculus: probability × severity vs cost and practicability of precautions.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: SAFE EQUIPMENT · LATIMER V AEC · SLIPPERY FLOOR · REASONABLE PRECAUTIONS · PROPORTIONALITY · FACTORY NOT REQUIRED TO CLOSE · RISK CALCULUS

Duty of Care — Employers.front: What is the employer's duty regarding competent fellow employees, and what does Hudson v Ridge Manufacturing Co [1957] illustrate?
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
The employer must exercise reasonable care in the **selection, retention, and supervision** of employees to ensure that employees do not pose a risk of harm to their colleagues.

**Hudson v Ridge Manufacturing Co [1957]:**
- A fellow employee had a known habit of playing dangerous practical jokes on colleagues — he had been reprimanded but not dismissed.
- He grabbed the claimant, threw him to the floor, and the claimant broke his wrist.
- **Held:** The employer was liable. The employer knew of the employee's dangerous behaviour (a 'persistent practical joker') and failed to take adequate steps to prevent it (warnings had not worked — dismissal may have been required). The employer breached the duty to provide competent fellow employees.

**Key principle:** If an employer knows (or ought to know) that an employee is a risk to colleagues, the employer must take action proportionate to the risk — including dismissal if lesser steps have proved ineffective. The duty is not merely to warn but to protect.

**Contrast with vicarious liability:** This is the employer's **direct** duty — liability flows from the employer's own failure to act, not merely from the act of the employee. Both can apply on the same facts.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: COMPETENT FELLOW EMPLOYEES · HUDSON V RIDGE · KNOWN DANGEROUS EMPLOYEE · EMPLOYER MUST ACT · WARNINGS INSUFFICIENT · DIRECT DUTY NOT VICARIOUS

Duty of Care — Employers.front: Wilsons and Clyde Coal Co v English [1938]
Duty of Care — Employers.issue: Whether the employer's duty of care to employees is personal and non-delegable, or whether the employer can discharge it by appointing a competent third party to perform safety functions.
Duty of Care — Employers.rule: The employer's duty to provide a safe system of work, safe premises, safe equipment, and competent staff is a personal, non-delegable duty. The employer cannot discharge this duty by delegating it to another — if the delegate is negligent, the employer remains liable.
Duty of Care — Employers.application: A coalmine employee was injured when descending the mine at the end of a shift because the mine's haulage system (which the employer was required by statute to operate safely) was defective. The employer argued it had delegated management of the system to a competent manager, and that the manager's negligence, not the employer's, was the cause. The House of Lords rejected this: the employer's duty was personal and could not be delegated. Delegation to a manager did not relieve the employer of liability.
Duty of Care — Employers.conclusion: Employer liable. Wilsons and Clyde Coal is the foundational authority for the employer's non-delegable four-component duty of care. It establishes that the employer's duty cannot be outsourced — the employer remains personally responsible for any failure.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: WILSONS AND CLYDE COAL 1938 · NON-DELEGABLE DUTY · FOUR COMPONENTS · SAFE PLACE · SAFE SYSTEM · SAFE EQUIPMENT · COMPETENT STAFF · HL AUTHORITY

Duty of Care — Employers.front: McDermid v Nash Dredging and Reclamation Co Ltd [1987]
Duty of Care — Employers.issue: Whether an employer is liable when an employee is injured by the negligence of an independent contractor engaged by the employer to carry out operations forming part of the employer's non-delegable duty.
Duty of Care — Employers.rule: The employer's duty to provide a safe system of work is non-delegable. If the employer delegates operation of a safe working system to an independent contractor who fails to implement it safely, the employer remains personally liable for the resulting injury.
Duty of Care — Employers.application: McDermid worked as a deckhand on a Dutch-owned tug, engaged by his employer Nash Dredging under an arrangement with a Dutch company. The captain of the Dutch tug (an independent contractor's employee) gave a signal to start engines before McDermid had finished work on deck. McDermid was swept overboard and lost his legs. Nash Dredging had delegated control of the safe system of working to the Dutch company. The House of Lords held Nash Dredging liable — the duty to provide a safe system of work was the employer's personal duty and could not be discharged by delegation.
Duty of Care — Employers.conclusion: Employer liable despite delegation to independent contractor. McDermid confirms and applies the Wilsons and Clyde Coal principle: the employer's duty to provide a safe system of work is non-delegable and cannot be outsourced.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: MCDERMID V NASH DREDGING 1987 · NON-DELEGABLE SAFE SYSTEM · INDEPENDENT CONTRACTOR DELEGATION INSUFFICIENT · EMPLOYER PERSONALLY LIABLE · HL AUTHORITY

Duty of Care — Employers.front: Yasmin works for CleanCo as a window cleaner. Her employer issues ladders but no training on ladder safety. Her supervisor, who is employed by CleanCo, told her the system is 'straightforward — just get on with it.' One day a ladder slips on a wet pavement and Yasmin falls, suffering a broken arm. She sues CleanCo. Advise her.
Duty of Care — Employers.answer:
Yasmin should sue CleanCo in negligence for breach of the employer's non-delegable duty of care.

**Components engaged:**
1. **Safe system of work** — CleanCo provided no training on ladder safety and gave no instructions on assessing wet or uneven surfaces. The supervisor's dismissive attitude ('just get on with it') indicates no proper system was in place. This is the primary breach.
2. **Safe equipment** — it should also be considered whether the ladders themselves were in good condition and whether non-slip feet or stabilisers should have been provided.

**Breach:**
- A reasonable employer providing window cleaning services would have implemented a safe system for ladder use, including training on wet-weather risks, surface checks, and stabiliser use.
- The failure to do so is a breach of the duty to provide a safe system of work: *Wilsons and Clyde Coal v English* [1938].

**Causation:** But for the failure to train Yasmin on wet-surface ladder safety, she would likely have been instructed to take precautions or to decline to use the ladder on the wet pavement. The breach caused her injury.

**Defences:** CleanCo might argue contributory negligence if Yasmin disregarded known risks, but without training she cannot reasonably be expected to have known the proper precautions.

**Conclusion:** Claim likely to succeed. Damages for the broken arm, plus any lost earnings during recovery.
Duty of Care — Employers.summary_line: EMPLOYER'S DUTY · SAFE SYSTEM OF WORK · NO TRAINING · WILSONS AND CLYDE COAL · BREACH · CAUSATION · CONTRIBUTORY NEGLIGENCE POSSIBLE

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: What is the standard of care against which breach of duty in negligence is assessed?
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer: The defendant is judged against the standard of the reasonable person — the 'man on the Clapham omnibus'. This is an objective standard: the defendant's personal characteristics, inexperience, or subjective beliefs are irrelevant (Blyth v Birmingham Waterworks Co [1856]). The standard is modified: (i) professionals are judged against the competent professional (Bolam); (ii) children are judged against the reasonable child of the same age (Mullin v Richards [1998]).
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: REASONABLE PERSON · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · BLYTH v BIRMINGHAM WATERWORKS · NOT SUBJECTIVE · MODIFIED FOR PROFESSIONALS AND CHILDREN

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: What factors does a court consider when assessing whether the defendant fell below the standard of the reasonable person?
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer:
The court weighs:

1. Probability of harm — greater risk requires more precaution (Bolton v Stone [1951] HL — low probability of cricket ball injury, no breach)
2. Magnitude of harm — greater potential harm requires more care (Paris v Stepney BC [1951] HL — one-eyed worker, greater duty)
3. Cost and practicability of precautions — disproportionate cost may mean no breach
4. Social utility — emergency services may take greater risks for legitimate purposes (Watt v Hertfordshire CC [1954])
5. Common practice — compliance is strong but not conclusive evidence of no breach.
