Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: BREACH FACTORS · PROBABILITY · MAGNITUDE · COST OF PRECAUTIONS · SOCIAL UTILITY · COMMON PRACTICE · BOLTON v STONE · PARIS v STEPNEY

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: What standard of care applies to a learner driver, and why?
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer:
A learner driver is held to the objective standard of a reasonably competent qualified driver (Nettleship v Weston [1971] CA). Their inexperience and learner status are irrelevant to the standard of care. Reasons:

1. Road users and passengers are entitled to expect all drivers will meet the same standard
2. Insurance is compulsory for motor vehicles, protecting those who suffer damage.

The principle generalises: whoever undertakes a skilled activity is judged against the competent practitioner, not against the standard of a beginner.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: LEARNER DRIVER · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · COMPETENT DRIVER · INEXPERIENCE IRRELEVANT · NETTLESHIP v WESTON · INSURANCE RATIONALE

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: What standard of care applies to a child defendant in negligence?
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer: A child defendant is judged against the standard of an ordinary, careful child of the same age (Mullin v Richards [1998] CA). In Mullin v Richards, two 15-year-old schoolgirls were playing with plastic rulers; one broke and a splinter blinded the claimant. The CA held no breach — it was not reasonably foreseeable to a 15-year-old that the activity was dangerous. This is a modified objective standard: the child is not judged against an adult's standard, but also not purely by their own subjective understanding.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: CHILD DEFENDANT · STANDARD OF ORDINARY CHILD OF SAME AGE · MODIFIED OBJECTIVE · MULLIN v RICHARDS · NOT ADULT STANDARD

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: What is the principle of res ipsa loquitur and when does it apply?
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer:
Res ipsa loquitur ('the thing speaks for itself') permits an inference of negligence from the mere occurrence of the accident, without the claimant needing to prove the specific breach. It applies where:

1. the thing causing damage was under the defendant's management or control
2. the accident is of a kind that would not ordinarily occur if reasonable care had been exercised.

Effect: the evidential burden shifts to the defendant to provide an explanation consistent with no negligence. If the defendant cannot do so, negligence may be inferred. It does not shift the legal burden of proof — the claimant still bears that overall.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: RES IPSA LOQUITUR · EVIDENTIAL PRINCIPLE · CONTROL · ACCIDENT NOT ORDINARY WITHOUT NEGLIGENCE · SHIFTS EVIDENTIAL BURDEN · NOT LEGAL BURDEN

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.issue: Whether a learner driver should be held to the standard of the competent qualified driver, or to a lower standard reflecting their inexperience.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.rule: A learner driver is held to the objective standard of the reasonably competent qualified driver. Personal characteristics (inexperience, learner status) are irrelevant to the standard of care. The rationale is that other road users and passengers are entitled to expect the same standard of driving from all drivers, and compulsory insurance protects those injured.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.application: Mr Nettleship was teaching Mrs Weston to drive. During a lesson, Mrs Weston failed to straighten the wheel after a bend, mounted a pavement, and injured Mr Nettleship. She argued her standard of care should be adjusted to reflect her inexperience. The CA rejected this.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.conclusion: Mrs Weston was negligent: she failed to reach the standard of the reasonably competent driver. Her contributory negligence was considered separately. The case confirms that whoever undertakes a skilled activity is judged against the competent practitioner, not against the standard of a beginner.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: LEARNER DRIVER · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · COMPETENT DRIVER · INEXPERIENCE IRRELEVANT · INSURANCE RATIONALE · ALL DRIVERS SAME STANDARD

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.issue: Whether a cricket club was negligent when a batsman hit a ball out of the ground and it struck and injured a person on the adjacent road.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.rule: In assessing breach, the probability of harm is a key factor. Where the risk of injury is very low — even if harm is foreseeable in theory — a defendant may not be negligent if the precautions required to eliminate the risk would be wholly disproportionate to it. Negligence involves an unreasonable failure to guard against risk.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.application: A cricket ball was hit 100 yards over a 17-foot fence at Old Trafford and struck Miss Stone on the road. Evidence showed balls had only cleared the fence six times in 28 years. The HL held the risk of injury was so small that the reasonable person would not have taken further precautions.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.conclusion: No negligence. The HL weighed the very low probability of injury against the burden of preventing it. Bolton v Stone is a leading case on the probability element of the breach enquiry — low risk can mean no breach even where harm is foreseeable in the abstract.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: PROBABILITY OF HARM · LOW RISK · NO BREACH · CRICKET BALL · COST OF PRECAUTIONS · RISK v BURDEN · MISS STONE

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: Paris v Stepney Borough Council [1951] HL
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.issue: Whether an employer was negligent in failing to provide goggles to a worker who was blind in one eye, where goggles were not routinely provided to workers with normal sight.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.rule: The magnitude of potential harm is a factor in assessing the standard of care required. Where a claimant is especially vulnerable and the consequences of injury would be disproportionately severe, a higher duty of precaution may be owed — even if the same precaution would not be required for others.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.application: Mr Paris had only one functional eye. A metal splinter entered his sighted eye while he worked without goggles; he became completely blind. The council did not provide goggles to any workers in this role. The HL held the council was in breach because, given Paris's particular vulnerability, goggles should have been provided to him specifically.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.conclusion: The council was negligent. The HL emphasised that the magnitude of harm — total blindness — justified requiring precautions that might not be necessary for an able-sighted worker. The case shows that reasonable care is calibrated to the risk as it actually presents itself to the defendant.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: MAGNITUDE OF HARM · ONE-EYED WORKER · GOGGLES · GREATER DUTY FOR VULNERABLE CLAIMANT · PARIS v STEPNEY · CALIBRATED TO ACTUAL RISK

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: Aisha, a junior doctor in her first week of practice, administers the wrong dosage of medication to a patient, Victor. Victor suffers an adverse reaction and is hospitalised for a further week. Aisha argues she should be judged against the standard of a newly qualified doctor, not an experienced one. Advise Victor.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer:
Standard of care: The standard of care in negligence is objective and is calibrated to the role undertaken, not to the individual's experience. A doctor is judged against the standard of the ordinarily competent doctor performing that function (Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957]; Nettleship v Weston [1971] CA — learner driver held to standard of competent driver).

Aisha's argument fails: she cannot rely on her inexperience to lower the standard. By holding herself out as a qualified doctor and taking on the role, she is held to the standard of the competent doctor in that position. This protects patients who are entitled to expect appropriate care regardless of who treats them.

Breach: Administering the wrong dosage — below the standard of the competent doctor — constitutes a breach.

Conclusion: Aisha is in breach of her duty. Victor has a strong negligence claim. Her hospital employer may also be vicariously liable.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: STANDARD OF CARE · OBJECTIVE · INEXPERIENCE IRRELEVANT · COMPETENT DOCTOR · NETTLESHIP · BOLAM · JUNIOR DOCTOR · BREACH

Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.subtopic: Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.front: A factory roof, weakened by years of neglect, collapses during a storm. A maintenance engineer, Stefan, was injured while working on the roof. The factory owner, Alderton Ltd, argues that no other factory in the industry fitted roof reinforcements as a precaution against storm damage, and that the storm was more severe than average. Advise Stefan.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.answer:
Breach — relevant factors:

1. Probability of harm: A weakened roof carries an ongoing risk of collapse, particularly in adverse weather. The probability is not negligible — this factor points toward breach.

2. Magnitude of harm: Roof collapse while a worker is present creates a risk of serious injury or death — high magnitude.

3. Common practice: Alderton argues that no other factory in the industry takes this precaution. Common practice is strong evidence against breach but not conclusive. A court can find breach even where common practice is followed, if that practice is itself negligent (Edward v National Coal Board [1949]).

4. Social utility: A commercial factory roof is not a high social utility activity that would justify accepting elevated risk.

5. Cost of precautions: Roof maintenance or reinforcement is a known and manageable cost.

Novus actus: The storm is a natural event — but the weakness of the roof was the defendant's fault. An ordinary (not exceptional) storm would not break the chain of causation if the roof was already structurally deficient.

Conclusion: Despite the industry practice, Alderton is likely in breach. A negligently maintained roof that collapses during a foreseeable storm is unlikely to be excused by common industry custom.
Breach of Duty — The Standard of Care.summary_line: BREACH FACTORS · PROBABILITY · MAGNITUDE · COMMON PRACTICE NOT CONCLUSIVE · COST OF PRECAUTIONS · STORM · NOVUS ACTUS · MAINTENANCE FAILURE

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: What is the Bolam test and to what situations does it apply?
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer: The Bolam test (Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] McNair J): a professional person is not negligent if they acted in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of practitioners in that profession, even if other practitioners would have acted differently. There can be two schools of professional opinion; adherence to either is sufficient. The test applies to all professional defendants but is most frequently used in clinical negligence. It covers diagnosis and treatment decisions, but NOT the disclosure of information to patients (Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] SC replaced Bolam in that context).
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BOLAM TEST · RESPONSIBLE BODY OF OPINION · TWO SCHOOLS · PROFESSIONAL NEGLIGENCE · CLINICAL NEGLIGENCE · MONTGOMERY EXCEPTION

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: What did Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] add to the Bolam test?
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer: Bolitho v City and Hackney HA [1998] HL: a professional body of opinion must also withstand logical analysis. The court is not bound to find no negligence merely because the defendant's conduct is supported by professional opinion. Expert opinion must be reasonable, responsible, and capable of withstanding logical scrutiny. A court can reject expert evidence if it is not logically defensible. In practice, successful Bolitho challenges are rare. Combined effect: a defendant acts non-negligently if a responsible and logical professional body would have acted the same way.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BOLITHO · BOLAM QUALIFIED · LOGICAL ANALYSIS · COURT NOT BOUND · REASONABLE AND RESPONSIBLE · RARE TO SUCCEED

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: How did Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] modify the application of the Bolam test?
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer:
Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] SC: a patient was not warned of the risk of shoulder dystocia in vaginal delivery. The Supreme Court held: Bolam does NOT apply to the disclosure of risks to patients. A doctor must take reasonable care to ensure the patient is aware of any material risks involved in proposed treatment and of reasonable alternative treatments. A risk is 'material' if a reasonable person in the patient's position would be likely to attach significance to it, or if the doctor is or should be aware that this particular patient would.

⚠️ Montgomery applies to disclosure of risk only — Bolam/Bolitho still governs diagnosis and treatment decisions.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: MONTGOMERY v LANARKSHIRE · DISCLOSURE OF RISK · NOT BOLAM · MATERIAL RISK · PATIENT-CENTRED · REPLACES SIDAWAY · BOLAM STILL FOR TREATMENT

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front:
Apply the combined Bolam/Bolitho test to the following scenario:

Dr Ahmed, a consultant surgeon, performs a procedure using Technique X. Expert evidence shows that 60% of surgeons use Technique Y (the more common approach), but a body of 40% use Technique X, which carries a small additional risk of nerve damage. The claimant suffers nerve damage. Dr Ahmed's experts say Technique X is acceptable. The claimant's experts say Technique X is not logically defensible given the documented nerve damage risk.

Work through the two-stage Bolam/Bolitho analysis.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer:
Stage 1 — Bolam: Is there a responsible body of professional opinion supporting Dr Ahmed's conduct?
Yes. 40% of surgeons use Technique X. A body of responsible practitioners endorses this approach. Under Bolam alone, Dr Ahmed would not be negligent — the existence of a supporting body of opinion is sufficient, even if the majority would have done otherwise.

Stage 2 — Bolitho: Does that body of opinion withstand logical analysis?
This is where the claimant's argument bites. The court must ask: is the 40% body of opinion reasonable, responsible, and capable of withstanding logical scrutiny? (Bolitho v City and Hackney HA [1998] HL.)
If the claimant's experts can demonstrate that Technique X carries a documented nerve damage risk that responsible practitioners would not accept — and that there is no logical basis for preferring it — the court can reject the 40% opinion as illogical.

Outcome: If Bolitho stage 2 is satisfied, Dr Ahmed is negligent despite the Bolam threshold being met. However, Bolitho challenges rarely succeed in practice — courts are slow to reject professional opinion as illogical. The claimant bears a high evidential burden at stage 2.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BOLAM/BOLITHO TWO STAGES · RESPONSIBLE BODY OF OPINION · LOGICAL ANALYSIS · BOLITHO CHALLENGE RARELY SUCCEEDS · PROFESSIONAL JUDGMENT · NERVE DAMAGE

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957] QB
Breach of Duty — Professionals.issue: What standard of care should be applied to a doctor (or other professional) when assessing negligence?
Breach of Duty — Professionals.rule: A doctor (or professional) is not negligent if they act in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of practitioners skilled in that field, even if other practitioners would have acted differently. There can be two schools of thought; following either is sufficient. The test is objective but calibrated to the competent professional.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.application: Mr Bolam consented to electro-convulsive therapy (ECT) but was not warned of the risk of fractures, nor physically restrained during treatment. He suffered serious injuries. There were two schools of opinion on whether to use relaxant drugs and restraints. McNair J directed the jury using the 'responsible body of medical opinion' test.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.conclusion: No negligence: the doctors had followed a practice accepted by a responsible body of medical opinion. The Bolam test was established as the standard for all professional negligence cases. The test has subsequently been qualified by Bolitho and, for disclosure of risk, replaced by the Montgomery standard.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BOLAM TEST · RESPONSIBLE BODY OF OPINION · TWO SCHOOLS SUFFICIENT · ECT · PROFESSIONAL STANDARD · QUALIFIED BY BOLITHO · REPLACED BY MONTGOMERY FOR DISCLOSURE

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] HL
Breach of Duty — Professionals.issue: Whether the Bolam test could be satisfied by expert evidence that did not withstand logical analysis.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.rule: A body of professional opinion relied on to satisfy the Bolam test must also be capable of withstanding logical analysis. The court is not bound to find no negligence merely because the defendant's conduct is supported by expert opinion. Where that opinion is not reasonable, responsible, or logically defensible, the court may reject it. In practice, successful Bolitho challenges are rare.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.application: A two-year-old boy suffered serious brain damage after respiratory difficulties. A doctor failed to attend. Even if she had attended, she said she would not have intubated. The question was whether failure to intubate would have been negligent. Her expert supported that view; the claimant's expert disagreed. The HL held the trial judge was entitled to prefer the claimant's expert — but on the facts, the defendant's expert evidence was not illogical.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.conclusion: The defendant was not liable on the facts. However, the HL confirmed that Bolam is qualified: a body of professional opinion must be capable of withstanding logical scrutiny. Bolitho is invoked where the expert evidence supporting the defendant is demonstrably illogical.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BOLAM QUALIFIED · LOGICAL ANALYSIS · COURT NOT BOUND BY EXPERT OPINION · RARE TO SUCCEED · REASONABLE AND RESPONSIBLE · BOLITHO CHALLENGE

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] SC
Breach of Duty — Professionals.issue: Whether the Bolam test applied to a doctor's duty to advise a patient of material risks of treatment and of alternative treatments.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.rule: The Bolam test does NOT apply to the disclosure of risks. A doctor must take reasonable care to ensure that a patient is aware of any material risks involved in proposed treatment, and of any reasonable alternatives. A risk is 'material' if a reasonable person in the patient's position would be likely to attach significance to it, or if the doctor knows or should know that this particular patient would attach significance to it.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.application: Mrs Montgomery, a diabetic, was not warned of the risk of shoulder dystocia (a risk of vaginal delivery) and was not offered a caesarean section. During birth her son suffered severe brain damage. The doctor's evidence was that she would not routinely have raised this risk as she believed patients would opt for caesarean if warned. The SC overruled the Sidaway approach (which applied Bolam to disclosure).
Breach of Duty — Professionals.conclusion: The Health Board was liable. The SC replaced the Bolam/Sidaway standard with a patient-centred standard for disclosure of risk. Montgomery applies only to disclosure; Bolam/Bolitho continue to govern diagnosis and treatment decisions.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: MONTGOMERY · DISCLOSURE OF RISK · NOT BOLAM · MATERIAL RISK · PATIENT-CENTRED · REASONABLE PATIENT · REPLACES SIDAWAY · DIAGNOSIS/TREATMENT STILL BOLAM

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: Dr Reza, a cardiologist, recommends a surgical procedure to patient Lin. He explains the procedure generally but does not mention a 2% risk of a specific complication — permanent nerve damage — because, in his clinical judgment, patients who are told about this risk become unnecessarily anxious and often refuse beneficial treatment. Lin undergoes the procedure and suffers the nerve damage. Advise Lin.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer:
Disclosure of risk — Montgomery: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] SC fundamentally changed the law on disclosure. The Bolam test does NOT apply to the disclosure of risks to patients.

The Montgomery standard: A doctor must ensure the patient is aware of any material risks. A risk is material if: (a) a reasonable person in the patient's position would be likely to attach significance to it; or (b) the doctor knows or ought to know this particular patient would attach significance to it.

Dr Reza's justification fails: A 2% risk of permanent nerve damage is a serious, life-altering consequence. A reasonable person would clearly want to know about this risk before consenting to surgery. The fact that patients may refuse treatment if informed does not permit a doctor to withhold information — patient autonomy requires that informed decisions be made by the patient, not by the doctor on their behalf (this was precisely the approach rejected in Montgomery).

Conclusion: Dr Reza breached his duty of care by failing to disclose the material risk. Lin must also prove causation — that, if warned, she would have declined the procedure or chosen an alternative. Lin has a strong Montgomery claim.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: MONTGOMERY · DISCLOSURE OF RISK · NOT BOLAM · MATERIAL RISK · PERMANENT NERVE DAMAGE · PATIENT AUTONOMY · 2% RISK MATERIAL · CAUSATION ALSO REQUIRED

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: A child, Bea (aged 9), is injured when she trips over a loose paving slab in the school playground. School inspections had noted the slab as 'requiring monitoring' six months earlier but no repair had been made. The school argues that two expert witnesses support its inspection regime as reasonable. Advise Bea's parents.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer:
Breach — standard of care: The school (as an institution) is judged against the standard of a reasonably competent school authority. This is not a pure Bolam case (Bolam applies to professional medical/skilled judgments); the question is whether the school met the standard of reasonable care in maintaining the premises.

Key facts: The loose slab was identified six months earlier and not repaired. 'Requiring monitoring' is not equivalent to taking action — the school had actual knowledge of a hazard and took no remedial steps.

Bolitho relevance: Even if expert evidence supports the monitoring approach, the court should ask whether that approach is logically defensible (Bolitho v City and Hackney HA [1998]). Leaving a known hazard unrepaired for six months — particularly in a playground used by young children — may not withstand logical scrutiny.

Employer's duty: As an occupier, the school owes a duty under OLA 1957 s.2(2) to keep visitors (pupils) reasonably safe. A known defect that was not remedied is strong evidence of breach.

Conclusion: The school is likely in breach both in negligence and under OLA 1957. The expert evidence supporting the monitoring regime may not be logically defensible given actual knowledge of the hazard.
Breach of Duty — Professionals.summary_line: BREACH · KNOWN HAZARD · SIX MONTHS · MONITORING INSUFFICIENT · BOLITHO · LOGICAL ANALYSIS · OLA 1957 · SCHOOL · CHILD VISITOR · EXPERT EVIDENCE

Breach of Duty — Professionals.front: Going deeper: Has Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] fundamentally changed the doctor-patient relationship, and has it gone far enough?
Breach of Duty — Professionals.answer:
Montgomery replaced the Bolam/Sidaway approach to disclosure with a patient-centred standard — requiring disclosure of any risk a reasonable patient in the claimant's position would regard as material, or that this particular patient would attach significance to.

The autonomy turn: Montgomery reflects a broader shift in medical law from medical paternalism to patient autonomy, influenced by the human rights framework (Article 8 ECHR — right to private life). Lord Kerr and Lord Reed drew explicitly on developments in other common law jurisdictions (Rogers v Whitaker [1992] HCA Australia; Canterbury v Spence [1972] DC — though not binding). The decision brought English law into line with most developed legal systems.

Therapeutic privilege: Montgomery preserves 'therapeutic privilege' — a doctor may withhold information if disclosure would cause the patient psychological harm that would itself be detrimental to health. Herring (Medical Law and Ethics, 2020) argues the privilege is too broad — it reintroduces paternalism through the back door and is difficult to police.

Causation difficulties: Claimants must prove they would have refused treatment or chosen an alternative if warned. Courts apply both an objective (reasonable patient) and subjective (this patient) test to causation. Montgomery cases frequently fail on causation even where the disclosure breach is established — undermining the practical utility of the right to informed consent.

Bolam retained: Montgomery applies only to disclosure of risk. Diagnosis and treatment decisions remain governed by Bolam/Bolitho. Some commentators (Brazier, 2016) argue this is a principled line; others argue it is inconsistent — why should patient autonomy govern risk-disclosure but not treatment choice?

Remaining gap: English law has no equivalent of the French doctrine of 'informed refusal' — a competent patient's right to refuse recommended treatment without explanation is protected by the MCA 2005 but is not framed as a tort duty on the doctor. Reform of this area is ongoing.

Causation — Factual Causation.front: What is the 'but for' test for factual causation in negligence?
Causation — Factual Causation.answer: The 'but for' test (Barnett v Chelsea & Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969]): the claimant must prove that but for the defendant's breach of duty, the damage would not have occurred. If the damage would have happened regardless of the breach, causation is not established and the claim fails. In Barnett, a patient was sent away without examination; even if he had been examined, he would have died from arsenical poisoning in any event — causation failed despite the doctor's breach. The test is applied on the balance of probabilities (>50%).
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: BUT FOR TEST · FACTUAL CAUSATION · BARNETT v CHELSEA · DAMAGE WOULD HAVE OCCURRED ANYWAY · BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES

Causation — Factual Causation.front: When is the material contribution to harm test used as an alternative to 'but for', and what is required?
Causation — Factual Causation.answer:
The material contribution test applies where multiple causative factors contributed to a single indivisible injury and it is scientifically impossible to isolate which factor caused the injury using the 'but for' test (Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] HL). The defendant is liable if their breach materially contributed — more than negligibly or trivially. In Bonnington, the claimant contracted pneumoconiosis from both 'guilty' (escapable) and 'innocent' dust. The employer was liable because the guilty dust materially contributed to the disease.

**Note:** material contribution to harm is distinct from the Fairchild 'material increase in risk' test.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: MATERIAL CONTRIBUTION TO HARM · BONNINGTON CASTINGS · INDIVISIBLE INJURY · BUT FOR INAPPLICABLE · MORE THAN NEGLIGIBLE · DISTINCT FROM FAIRCHILD

Causation — Factual Causation.front: What is the Fairchild exception to the usual rules of factual causation, and how does the Compensation Act 2006 interact with it?
Causation — Factual Causation.answer: Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] HL: where a claimant is exposed to a noxious agent by multiple defendants, each in breach of duty, and it is impossible to attribute which defendant's breach caused the disease (mesothelioma), a defendant who materially increased the risk of injury is treated as having materially contributed to it. Barker v Corus UK [2006] HL held each employer liable only proportionately. Parliament reversed this: s.3 Compensation Act 2006 provides that a defendant liable for negligently causing mesothelioma is jointly and severally liable for the whole damage. The Fairchild exception is currently confined to mesothelioma/asbestos cases.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: FAIRCHILD · MATERIAL INCREASE IN RISK · MESOTHELIOMA · BARKER v CORUS · COMPENSATION ACT 2006 S.3 · JOINT AND SEVERAL

Causation — Factual Causation.front: The 'but for' test breaks down in three distinct scenarios. For each, identify the problem with 'but for' and state the alternative causation approach that applies.
Causation — Factual Causation.answer:
1. Multiple sufficient independent causes — e.g., two separate fires, each alone sufficient to destroy the claimant's property, combine to cause the damage. Applying 'but for', neither fire is the cause (the damage would have occurred from the other). Solution: courts treat either cause as sufficient causation; the defendant is liable if their breach was a cause (Baker v Willoughby [1970] HL approach; also Lambeth v Cee [independent causes principle]).

2. Indivisible injury from multiple causative factors — multiple defendants/sources each contribute to a single indivisible disease or injury, but it is scientifically impossible to say which source caused it (e.g., dust from multiple sources causing lung disease). Applying 'but for' strictly, no single defendant can be shown to have caused the injury. Solution: material contribution to harm test — the defendant is liable if their breach materially contributed (more than negligibly) to the injury (Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956]).

3. Causal mechanism scientifically unknown — multiple defendants each exposed the claimant to the same risk (asbestos/mesothelioma), but it is scientifically impossible to attribute the disease to any one exposure. 'But for' cannot be applied at all. Solution: Fairchild exception — a defendant who materially increased the risk of the disease is treated as having caused it (Fairchild v Glenhaven [2002]; joint and several liability confirmed by Compensation Act 2006 s.3).
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: BUT FOR BREAKS DOWN · THREE SCENARIOS · MULTIPLE SUFFICIENT CAUSES · MATERIAL CONTRIBUTION · FAIRCHILD · BONNINGTON CASTINGS · CA 2006 S.3

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Barnett v Chelsea & Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969] QB
Causation — Factual Causation.issue: Whether the hospital's breach of duty in failing to examine a patient was the cause of his death.
Causation — Factual Causation.rule: The 'but for' test: the claimant must prove that but for the defendant's breach of duty, the damage would not have occurred. If the damage would have happened regardless of the breach, the causation element is not established and the claim fails — even if there is a clear breach of duty.
Causation — Factual Causation.application: Three night-watchmen attended Chelsea & Kensington Hospital after drinking tea that had been poisoned with arsenic. The duty doctor refused to examine them, telling them to go home and see their own doctors. Mr Barnett died. However, medical evidence showed that even if he had been examined and treated, he would have died from arsenical poisoning because the dose was lethal and antidotes were not then available.
Causation — Factual Causation.conclusion: The claim failed on causation. Despite a clear breach of duty (failure to examine), the 'but for' test was not satisfied — the claimant would have died in any event. This is the leading illustration of the but-for test defeating a negligence claim.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: BUT FOR TEST · CAUSATION FAILS DESPITE BREACH · ARSENIC · DAMAGE WOULD HAVE OCCURRED ANYWAY · BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] HL
Causation — Factual Causation.issue: Whether the employer's breach (allowing some, but not all, of the dust causing the claimant's disease) was the legal cause of the disease, when the but-for test could not be applied.
Causation — Factual Causation.rule: Where an indivisible disease is caused by a combination of guilty (actionable) and innocent (non-actionable) factors, a defendant is liable if their breach materially contributed to the harm — i.e., more than negligibly or trivially. The 'material contribution to harm' test applies where the but-for test cannot isolate individual causes of an indivisible injury.
Causation — Factual Causation.application: Mr Wardlaw contracted pneumoconiosis from inhaling silica dust at his foundry. Some dust came from swing grinders (breach — no ventilation) and some from pneumatic hammers (not a breach). The HL held it was impossible to attribute the disease to one source or the other. But the guilty dust materially contributed to the disease.
Causation — Factual Causation.conclusion: The employer was liable. The guilty dust's material contribution to the indivisible disease was sufficient for causation. This is the leading authority on material contribution to harm — distinct from Fairchild's material increase in risk.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: MATERIAL CONTRIBUTION TO HARM · INDIVISIBLE DISEASE · GUILTY v INNOCENT DUST · BUT FOR INAPPLICABLE · MORE THAN NEGLIGIBLE · DISTINCT FROM FAIRCHILD

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services Ltd [2002] HL
Causation — Factual Causation.issue: Whether, where a claimant had been exposed to asbestos by multiple employers over time — and it was scientifically impossible to say which employer's exposure caused the mesothelioma — any employer could be held liable.
Causation — Factual Causation.rule: Where the claimant was exposed to a noxious agent by multiple defendants each in breach of duty, and causal science means it is impossible to say which exposure caused the disease, a defendant who materially increased the risk of the disease is treated as having materially contributed to it. This is an exceptional departure from the but-for test, currently confined to mesothelioma/asbestos cases.
Causation — Factual Causation.application: The claimants had each worked for several employers who had negligently exposed them to asbestos. Mesothelioma is caused by a single fibre and it cannot be scientifically determined which employer's asbestos caused the tumour. The HL held the but-for test could not be applied and adopted the 'material increase in risk' approach.
Causation — Factual Causation.conclusion: Each employer who materially increased the risk was liable. Barker v Corus [2006] later held proportionate liability, but the Compensation Act 2006 s.3 reversed this for mesothelioma — each defendant is now jointly and severally liable for the full damage.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: MATERIAL INCREASE IN RISK · MESOTHELIOMA · ASBESTOS · BUT FOR INAPPLICABLE · FAIRCHILD EXCEPTION · BARKER v CORUS · CA 2006 S.3 · JOINT AND SEVERAL

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Tom is admitted to hospital with suspected appendicitis. Nurse Ellie negligently fails to pass on Tom's test results to the on-call doctor. Tom's appendix ruptures overnight. However, medical evidence shows that even if the results had been passed on, the on-call doctor was so overwhelmed that night that Tom would not have been operated on until morning — when the rupture would have occurred in any event. Advise Tom.
Causation — Factual Causation.answer:
Factual causation — but for test: Tom must prove that but for Ellie's breach, he would not have suffered the rupture (Barnett v Chelsea & Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969]).

Applying but for: Even if Ellie had passed on the results, the medical evidence shows the on-call doctor would not have operated before morning. The rupture would have occurred regardless. The but-for test is NOT satisfied.

Alternative causation approaches: There is no multiple independent cause or evidential gap situation here that would engage Bonnington Castings or Fairchild. The scenario presents a straightforward but-for failure.

Conclusion: Tom's claim fails on causation despite a clear breach of duty by Ellie. This mirrors the reasoning in Barnett — the damage would have occurred even without the breach. This is a harsh but legally correct result.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: BUT FOR TEST · CAUSATION FAILS DESPITE BREACH · BARNETT v CHELSEA · DAMAGE WOULD HAVE OCCURRED ANYWAY · NO ALTERNATIVE TEST AVAILABLE

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Maya worked for three different employers between 2005 and 2020, each of whom negligently exposed her to asbestos. She has now been diagnosed with mesothelioma. Scientific evidence cannot determine which employer's asbestos caused the tumour. Employer B has since become insolvent. Advise Maya.
Causation — Factual Causation.answer:
Fairchild exception: Where multiple defendants each negligently exposed the claimant to asbestos, and science cannot attribute the mesothelioma to any single exposure, each defendant who materially increased the risk is treated as having materially contributed to the harm (Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services [2002] HL).

Joint and several liability: Barker v Corus UK [2006] HL had held each defendant liable only for a proportionate share. However, s.3 Compensation Act 2006 reversed this for mesothelioma: any defendant who is liable for negligently causing a claimant to contract mesothelioma is jointly and severally liable for the whole damage. This means Maya can recover 100% of her damages from either Employer A or Employer C alone.

Employer B's insolvency: Under joint and several liability, Maya can pursue Employers A and C for the full amount. They cannot reduce their liability on account of Employer B's insolvency — that risk falls on the solvent defendants, not the claimant.

Conclusion: Maya should bring her claim against Employers A and C. Each is jointly and severally liable for the full damage under CA 2006 s.3. Employer B's insolvency does not prejudice her recovery.
Causation — Factual Causation.summary_line: FAIRCHILD · MESOTHELIOMA · MATERIAL INCREASE IN RISK · CA 2006 S.3 · JOINT AND SEVERAL LIABILITY · INSOLVENT DEFENDANT · FULL RECOVERY

Causation — Factual Causation.front: Going deeper: Has the material contribution and Fairchild exception produced a principled or unprincipled law of causation in tort?
Causation — Factual Causation.answer:
The orthodox but-for test is logically rigorous but produces unjust results in cases of evidential uncertainty. The courts have responded with exceptions that have attracted conflicting academic assessment.

Material contribution to harm (Bonnington [1956]): Operates where cumulative exposures produce an indivisible injury and the defendant's guilty contribution cannot be isolated. Widely accepted as principled.

Fairchild exception ([2002]): Lord Bingham justified it by reference to justice, fairness, and policy — not orthodox causation principle. This troubled commentators. Stapleton (2002) argued Fairchild was unprincipled and would be difficult to contain. Barker v Corus [2006] attempted containment by limiting each defendant to proportionate liability, but Parliament reversed this for mesothelioma within months (CA 2006 s.3). The result is a statutory rule of joint and several liability that is explicitly non-causal in character.

Gregg v Scott [2005]: The HL refused to extend Fairchild-type reasoning to loss of chance in clinical negligence. Lord Hoffmann's reasoning was that allowing percentage-chance claims would transform negligence from a 'balance of probabilities' system into a probabilistic one. Lord Nicholls (dissenting) argued this produced injustice: a claimant who lost a 45% chance of survival recovers nothing. Baroness Hale noted the incoherence with Hotson v East Berkshire AHA [1987].

Wider debate: Goldberg and Zipursky (Recognising Wrongs, 2020) argue causation doctrine has been distorted by outcome-driven reasoning. Wright's NESS test (Necessary Element of a Sufficient Set) offers a theoretically robust alternative to but-for, but has not been adopted by English courts.

Conclusion: The law of causation is a patchwork — principled at the centre (but-for), exceptional at the margins (Fairchild), and judicially contested at the boundaries (Gregg v Scott). Reform has come from Parliament rather than the courts.

Causation — Remoteness.front: What is the test for remoteness of damage in negligence, and what case established it?
Causation — Remoteness.answer: The Wagon Mound (No 1) test (Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock [1961] PC): a defendant is only liable for damage of a kind or type that was reasonably foreseeable at the time of the breach. The precise manner and extent of the damage need not be foreseeable, but the type must be. In the Wagon Mound, oil was negligently spilled into Sydney Harbour. Sparks set the oil alight, burning the claimant's wharf. The PC held: fire damage of that kind was not foreseeable from an oil spill — no liability.
Causation — Remoteness.summary_line: WAGON MOUND · REMOTENESS · REASONABLE FORESEEABILITY OF TYPE · KIND OF DAMAGE · EXTENT IRRELEVANT · MANNER IRRELEVANT

Causation — Remoteness.front: What is the thin skull (eggshell skull) rule and how does it interact with the Wagon Mound remoteness test?
Causation — Remoteness.answer: Once the type of damage is reasonably foreseeable, the defendant must take the claimant as they find them — including any pre-existing physical or psychological vulnerability (Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] QB). The extent of the damage does not need to be foreseeable. In Smith v Leech Brain, a minor burn (foreseeable) triggered cancer in pre-malignant tissue. The employer was held liable for the cancer, even though that extent of harm was unforeseeable. The thin skull rule operates alongside Wagon Mound: once a type of damage is foreseeable, the full extent is recoverable even if exacerbated by the claimant's pre-existing condition.
Causation — Remoteness.summary_line: THIN SKULL RULE · EGGSHELL SKULL · SMITH v LEECH BRAIN · TAKE CLAIMANT AS YOU FIND THEM · EXTENT NOT FORESEEABLE · TYPE MUST BE FORESEEABLE

Causation — Remoteness.front: Overseas Tankship (UK) Ltd v Morts Dock and Engineering Co Ltd (The Wagon Mound No 1) [1961] PC
Causation — Remoteness.issue: Whether a defendant is liable in negligence for damage of a type that was not reasonably foreseeable at the time of the breach, even if the breach could be shown to have caused the damage.
Causation — Remoteness.rule: A defendant is only liable for damage of a kind or type that was reasonably foreseeable at the time of the breach (the remoteness test). The precise manner and extent of the damage need not be foreseeable; only the type or kind. If the type was not foreseeable, no liability arises however serious the harm.
Causation — Remoteness.application: The defendants' employees negligently discharged oil from the Wagon Mound into Sydney Harbour. The oil drifted to the claimants' wharf and was ignited by welding sparks, setting fire to ships and the wharf. The Privy Council held that while the fouling of the harbour by oil was foreseeable, fire damage from the oil on water was not foreseeable at the time.
Causation — Remoteness.conclusion: No liability for the fire damage. The Wagon Mound replaced the Re Polemis direct consequence test with the foreseeability of type test. It remains the leading authority on remoteness of damage in negligence.
Causation — Remoteness.summary_line: WAGON MOUND · REMOTENESS · FORESEEABILITY OF TYPE · KIND OF DAMAGE · MANNER AND EXTENT IRRELEVANT · REPLACED RE POLEMIS · FIRE DAMAGE NOT FORESEEABLE

Causation — Remoteness.front: Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] QB
Causation — Remoteness.issue: Whether a defendant was liable for the full extent of the claimant's damage — cancer caused by a burn — when cancer was not a foreseeable consequence of the negligent act.
Causation — Remoteness.rule: The thin skull (eggshell skull) rule: once the type of damage is reasonably foreseeable, the defendant must take the claimant as they find them. The defendant is liable for the full extent of the harm, including harm exacerbated by a pre-existing vulnerability, even if that extent was not foreseeable.
Causation — Remoteness.application: A worker suffered a burn to his lip from molten metal — a foreseeable consequence of the defendant employer's negligence. The burn triggered cancer in pre-malignant tissue in his lip. He died. The employer argued that the cancer was too remote. Parker LJ held: the burn itself was foreseeable (type of harm established); the defendant must take the claimant as they find him, including his pre-malignant condition.
Causation — Remoteness.conclusion: The employer was liable for the cancer and death, not merely for the burn. The thin skull rule applies alongside the Wagon Mound test: foreseeability of type + take claimant as you find them = full liability for extent.
Causation — Remoteness.summary_line: THIN SKULL RULE · EGGSHELL SKULL · TAKE CLAIMANT AS YOU FIND · FORESEEABLE TYPE + UNFORESEEN EXTENT · BURN → CANCER · PRE-EXISTING VULNERABILITY

Causation — Remoteness.front: Gerry negligently breaks Isla's arm in a road accident. Isla is taken to hospital, where a surgeon performs an operation to reset the bone. Due to a rare complication that the surgeon could not have foreseen, Isla suffers permanent nerve damage in her arm. Gerry argues he is only liable for the broken arm, not the nerve damage. Advise Isla.
Causation — Remoteness.answer:
Thin skull rule: Once the type of damage is foreseeable, the defendant must take the claimant as they find them — including any pre-existing condition or vulnerability — and is liable for the full extent of the harm (Smith v Leech Brain & Co Ltd [1962] QB; Wagon Mound No 1 [1961] PC).

Type of damage: Physical injury from the road accident was clearly foreseeable. Gerry's breach caused a broken arm — a foreseeable type of harm.

Extent of harm: The permanent nerve damage is an extension of the physical injury caused by Gerry's negligence (through the medical treatment necessitated by it). The fact that the nerve damage arose from a rare, unforeseeable surgical complication does not break the chain. Medical treatment that is a direct response to the defendant's tort is not generally a novus actus interveniens unless it is grossly negligent (Robinson v Post Office [1974]).

Conclusion: Gerry is liable for the nerve damage. The type of harm (physical injury) was foreseeable; the thin skull rule makes him responsible for the full extent, including the surgical complication.
Causation — Remoteness.summary_line: THIN SKULL RULE · SMITH v LEECH BRAIN · FORESEEABLE TYPE · UNFORESEEN EXTENT · MEDICAL TREATMENT NOT NOVUS ACTUS · ROBINSON v POST OFFICE

Causation — Intervening Acts.front: What is a novus actus interveniens and what must be shown for it to break the chain of causation?
Causation — Intervening Acts.answer:
A novus actus interveniens (new intervening act) breaks the causal chain so the defendant is not liable for damage following the intervention. To break the chain, the intervening act must be:

1. truly independent of the original breach
2. unforeseeable
3. voluntary (in the case of a third party's act).

A foreseeable intervention generally does not break the chain. Three categories: (i) voluntary act of a third party; (ii) unreasonable and voluntary act of the claimant; (iii) natural event of unusual severity. Medical treatment that grossly and negligently worsens an injury may be a novus actus.
Causation — Intervening Acts.summary_line: NOVUS ACTUS INTERVENIENS · CHAIN OF CAUSATION · INDEPENDENT · UNFORESEEABLE · VOLUNTARY · THIRD PARTY · CLAIMANT · NATURAL EVENT

Causation — Intervening Acts.front: Donna negligently leaves a fire lit and unattended in her workshop. A stranger, unknown to Donna, enters the workshop and deliberately pours accelerant on the fire, causing it to spread and destroy neighbouring premises owned by Keith. Keith wishes to sue Donna. Advise Keith.
Causation — Intervening Acts.answer:
Novus actus interveniens — third party: The question is whether the stranger's deliberate act breaks the chain of causation between Donna's breach and Keith's loss.

Test: A third party's voluntary and unforeseeable act will break the chain of causation (novus actus interveniens). If the intervention is foreseeable, it generally does not break the chain.

Here: Donna left a fire lit and unattended in a workshop. Is it foreseeable that a stranger might interfere with an unattended fire? This is a difficult question. A deliberate act of arson (pouring accelerant) by a stranger is generally not a foreseeable consequence of leaving a fire unattended — it is an independent, voluntary, and criminal act. This is a stronger case for novus actus than mere negligence by a third party.

Contrast: If Donna had left the fire unattended and a gust of wind caused it to spread, no novus actus would arise.

Conclusion: The stranger's deliberate intervention is likely a novus actus interveniens that breaks the chain of causation. Keith's claim against Donna is likely to fail. He may have a direct claim against the stranger in negligence or under Rylands v Fletcher principles if the stranger can be identified.
Causation — Intervening Acts.summary_line: NOVUS ACTUS INTERVENIENS · DELIBERATE THIRD PARTY ACT · BREAKS CHAIN · FORESEEABILITY · ARSON · UNATTENDED FIRE · CAUSAL CHAIN · KEITH v DONNA

Damage.front: What types of damage are recoverable in a negligence claim, and what is required for the tort to be complete?
Damage.answer:
Unlike trespass (actionable per se), negligence requires proof of actual damage. Recoverable damage includes:

1. Personal injury — physical and recognised psychiatric injury
2. Property damage — physical damage to the claimant's property
3. Consequential economic loss — financial loss flowing directly from personal injury or property damage
4. Pure economic loss — NOT recoverable as a general rule (Spartan Steel), subject to the Hedley Byrne exception.

The damage must not be too remote (Wagon Mound test). The claimant must prove damage on the balance of probabilities.
Damage.summary_line: DAMAGE REQUIRED · NOT ACTIONABLE PER SE · PERSONAL INJURY · PROPERTY DAMAGE · CONSEQUENTIAL ECONOMIC LOSS · PURE ECONOMIC LOSS EXCLUDED

Duty of Care — Rescuers.front: Does a defendant who negligently creates a dangerous situation owe a duty of care to a rescuer who comes to assist?
Duty of Care — Rescuers.answer: Yes. A rescuer is a foreseeable person who will come to the assistance of someone endangered by the defendant's negligence. A duty of care is owed to professional and amateur rescuers who suffer injury attempting a reasonable rescue (Chadwick v British Railways Board [1967] — psychiatric harm suffered by rescuer at Lewisham rail crash). Volenti does not apply to rescuers. However, professional rescuers (police, fire brigade) who suffer only psychiatric harm from witnessing harm to others — without being in physical danger — are treated as secondary victims (White v Chief Constable of South Yorkshire [1999] HL) and must satisfy the Alcock criteria.
Duty of Care — Rescuers.summary_line: RESCUERS · DUTY OF CARE · FORESEEABLE RESCUER · CHADWICK v BRB · VOLENTI DOES NOT APPLY · WHITE v CHIEF CONSTABLE · SECONDARY VICTIMS

Causation — Loss of Chance.front: Is loss of a chance of a better medical outcome recoverable in negligence?
Causation — Loss of Chance.answer:
The general rule after Gregg v Scott [2005] HL (3:2 majority): loss of a chance of a better medical outcome is NOT recoverable. The claim must be analysed on the balance of probabilities — if on that balance the treatment would not have prevented the damage, the claim fails (Hotson v East Berkshire AHA [1987] HL). In Gregg v Scott, the HL declined to extend loss of a chance to medical cases. By contrast, loss of a chance is recoverable in commercial cases where the opportunity had a clear financial value (Allied Maples Group v Simmons & Simmons [1995] CA — loss of chance to negotiate with a third party).

⚠️ The law remains uncertain and has been criticised.
Causation — Loss of Chance.summary_line: LOSS OF CHANCE · GREGG v SCOTT · NOT RECOVERABLE IN MEDICAL · HOTSON · ALLIED MAPLES · COMMERCIAL LOSS OF CHANCE · ⚠️ UNCERTAIN

Psychiatric Harm — Secondary Victims.front: Following Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1, can a secondary victim recover for psychiatric harm suffered after witnessing a medical crisis caused by a prior clinical omission? Contrast this with witnessing a road accident caused by negligent driving.
Psychiatric Harm — Secondary Victims.answer:
Medical crisis following prior omission — NOT recoverable: Paul v Royal Wolverhampton NHS Trust [2024] UKSC 1 clarified that the duty of care owed by medical professionals to their patients does not extend to protecting family members from psychiatric harm caused by witnessing a medical crisis (e.g. a collapse or death) that results from an earlier failure to diagnose or treat. The traumatic event (the collapse) is not a 'sudden external accident' in the tortious sense — it is the downstream consequence of the patient's underlying condition. The Alcock secondary victim criteria (close tie, proximity, unaided senses) are retained but cannot be satisfied in this type of medical negligence scenario.

Road accident — potentially recoverable: Where the negligence is a discrete external accident (e.g. a car crash caused by a negligent driver), and a secondary victim witnesses that accident or its immediate aftermath, the Alcock criteria continue to apply in the ordinary way. If all three control mechanisms are satisfied, a secondary victim claim remains available.

The SQE 'lag' trap: Examiners frequently insert a time gap between the negligent act and the shocking event (e.g. 'the GP failed to prescribe medication three days earlier; the claimant's son collapsed at home'). This gap signals a medical omission scenario — Paul bars the secondary victim claim. No such bar applies where the shocking event IS the accident itself.

Summary: Accident (discrete external event) → Alcock applies. Medical omission → no secondary victim claim under Paul.
Psychiatric Harm — Secondary Victims.summary_line: PAUL [2024] · ACCIDENT vs MEDICAL CRISIS · OMISSION → NO SECONDARY VICTIM · ALCOCK RETAINED · SQE LAG TRAP · DISCRETE EXTERNAL ACCIDENT REQUIRED

Psychiatric Harm — Secondary Victims.front: Going deeper: Are the Alcock control mechanisms for secondary victims coherent, and should they be reformed?
Psychiatric Harm — Secondary Victims.answer:
The Alcock control mechanisms —

1. close tie of love and affection
2. proximity in time and space
3. direct perception through unaided senses — have attracted sustained academic criticism since their formulation in 1992.

The core incoherence: The mechanisms produce arbitrary outcomes that are difficult to defend on principle. A parent who watches their child die at the scene recovers; a parent who arrives minutes later does not. A spouse is presumed to have a close tie; a long-term partner (unmarried) must prove one. Lord Oliver in Alcock himself described the law as 'entirely arbitrary'. Stapleton
1994. argued the control mechanisms operate as a proxy for distributive justice concerns (limiting liability to a manageable class) rather than as principled legal rules.

Paul v Royal Wolverhampton [2024]: The Supreme Court retained Alcock but added a further restriction in the clinical negligence context: the shocking event must be a discrete external accident, not the downstream consequence of a prior medical omission. This has been criticised as creating a two-tier system with no clear doctrinal basis — why should the type of underlying negligence determine the secondary victim's right to recover?

Law Commission: The Law Commission (Report No 249, 1998) recommended reform — abolishing the close ties presumption list and replacing it with a general requirement of 'a relationship of love and affection' to be proved in each case. It also recommended relaxing the perception requirement. These recommendations were never implemented.

Comparative: Scots law and many civilian systems impose no equivalent proximity control mechanisms — the question is simply whether psychiatric injury was a foreseeable consequence of the defendant's breach. English law's unique rigidity has been attributed to floodgates concerns that have not materialised elsewhere.

The unresolved question: Whether the mechanisms should be replaced by a single foreseeability test (as in negligence generally) or retained as a policy-based filter. Given Parliament's inaction since 1998 and the Supreme Court's conservative approach in Paul, reform seems unlikely in the near term.

OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.front: What duty does an occupier owe to lawful visitors under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1957?
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.answer:
Under s.2(2) OLA 1957, an occupier owes a 'common duty of care' to all lawful visitors: to take such care as in all the circumstances is reasonable to see that the visitor will be reasonably safe in using the premises for the purposes for which they are invited or permitted to be there. Key points:

1. The duty is to keep the visitor reasonably safe, not necessarily to keep the premises safe
2. The duty extends to the purposes of the visit
3. 'Lawful visitor' includes those with express or implied permission and those with a legal right of entry.
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.summary_line: COMMON DUTY OF CARE · OLA 1957 · S.2(2) · LAWFUL VISITORS · REASONABLY SAFE · PURPOSE OF VISIT

OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.front:
For each scenario, identify whether the person is a lawful visitor (OLA 1957) or a trespasser (OLA 1984) at the time of injury, and why.

A. A plumber is engaged to fix a leak in a commercial kitchen. While waiting for parts, he wanders into the locked server room and falls.
B. A customer enters a supermarket during opening hours and slips on a wet floor in the main aisle.
C. A child climbs through a gap in a fence surrounding an electricity substation after seeing others do so.
D. A delivery driver, having made a delivery to the front door, takes a shortcut through the back garden without permission.
E. A tenant's friend visits the tenant's flat with the tenant's permission, though unknown to the landlord.
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.answer:
A. Trespasser (OLA 1984) — the plumber's implied permission extends to areas reasonably connected with the job. The locked server room is outside the scope of his invitation. He is a trespasser there.

B. Lawful visitor (OLA 1957) — a customer entering during opening hours has implied permission to be in the public areas of the shop.

C. Trespasser (OLA 1984) — a child who climbs through a fence enters without permission or legal right. However, under s.1(3) OLA 1984, the occupier may still owe a duty if the three conditions are met, particularly where children are known to access the area.

D. Trespasser (OLA 1984) — the delivery driver's implied permission extends to the front door for the purpose of delivery. The back garden is outside that permission; he is a trespasser there.

E. Lawful visitor (OLA 1957) — a friend invited by the tenant has implied permission from the occupier-in-possession (the tenant). The landlord's knowledge is irrelevant; it is the occupier who grants permission.
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.summary_line: OLA 1957 vs OLA 1984 · VISITOR OR TRESPASSER · SCOPE OF PERMISSION · IMPLIED LICENCE · DELIVERY DRIVER · CHILD · LOCKED ROOM

OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.front: Carla visits a supermarket owned by Freshmart Ltd. She slips on a patch of water near the fresh produce section and breaks her wrist. The water had been there for 45 minutes before the accident. A 'wet floor' sign was placed nearby but at an angle that made it difficult to see. Advise Carla.
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.answer:
OLA 1957: Carla is a lawful visitor (implied permission to enter during opening hours). Freshmart owes her the common duty of care under s.2(2) OLA 1957: to take such care as in all the circumstances is reasonable to see that she will be reasonably safe using the premises for the purposes of her visit.

Breach: The water had been present for 45 minutes — sufficient time for a reasonable inspection to have identified and dealt with it. The wet floor sign was placed at an angle making it difficult to see — a sign that cannot be seen cannot discharge the duty.

Warning notices: Under s.2(4)(a) OLA 1957, a warning can discharge the duty — but only if it is sufficient to enable the visitor to be reasonably safe. An obscured sign is unlikely to be sufficient.

Conclusion: Freshmart is likely in breach of the common duty of care. The 45-minute gap without inspection and the inadequate warning sign both support Carla's claim. She should also consider whether Freshmart's cleaning schedule was adequate.
OLA 1957 — Lawful Visitors.summary_line: OLA 1957 · COMMON DUTY OF CARE · S.2(2) · SUPERMARKET · SLIP · 45 MINUTES · WARNING SIGN INADEQUATE · S.2(4)(a) · BREACH

OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.front: How does the OLA 1957 address the special position of child visitors?
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.answer: Under s.2(3)(a) OLA 1957, an occupier must expect that children will be less careful than adults, and must guard accordingly. An occupier must guard against 'allurements' — things that would attract children but are dangerous to them. The leading case on allurements is Jolley v Sutton London Borough Council [2000] HL — an abandoned boat was a foreseeable allurement; the council was liable when a child was injured jacking it up. However, the occupier is entitled to assume that parents of very young children will take proper care of them (Phipps v Rochester Corporation [1955]). The overall question is what was reasonable in all the circumstances.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.summary_line: CHILD VISITORS · S.2(3)(a) OLA 1957 · LESS CAREFUL · ALLUREMENTS · JOLLEY v SUTTON [2000] · PHIPPS v ROCHESTER · PARENTAL SUPERVISION

OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.front: How does the OLA 1957 deal with skilled visitors exercising their trade or calling?
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.answer: Under s.2(3)(b) OLA 1957, an occupier may expect that a person in the exercise of a calling will appreciate and guard against any special risks ordinarily incident to that calling, so far as the occupier leaves them free to do so. A skilled contractor (electrician, roofer) should guard against ordinary professional risks themselves. However, the occupier retains the duty of care for matters outside the contractor's specialism and for structural dangers not obvious to someone of that trade (Roles v Nathan [1963] CA — chimney sweeps warned of risks; occupier not liable when they ignored the warning).
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.summary_line: SKILLED VISITORS · S.2(3)(b) OLA 1957 · EXERCISE OF CALLING · ORDINARY PROFESSIONAL RISKS · ROLES v NATHAN

OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.front: Jolley v Sutton London Borough Council [2000] HL
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.issue: Whether a local authority was liable under OLA 1957 when a child was seriously injured while attempting to repair a derelict boat the council had left on its land.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.rule: An occupier must guard against 'allurements' — things which attract children onto land and are dangerous. The kind of accident need not be precisely foreseen; it is sufficient that damage of the broad type was foreseeable. Courts should be slow to draw fine distinctions between foreseeable and unforeseeable types of mischief when children are involved.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.application: The council left an abandoned boat on its land. Two 14-year-old boys decided to repair it. They jacked up the boat to work underneath; it fell on one of them, causing serious spinal injuries. The council had planned to remove the boat. The CA found no liability (the precise accident was unforeseeable). The HL reversed this: it was foreseeable that children would meddle with the boat in ways that might cause injury.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.conclusion: The council was liable. The HL emphasised the wide scope of foreseeability when children and allurements are involved. Jolley is the leading case on child allurements and is important for illustrating how remoteness and breach interact in the occupier context.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.summary_line: CHILD ALLUREMENTS · OLA 1957 · DERELICT BOAT · WIDE FORESEEABILITY · CHILDREN AND MEDDLING · SPINAL INJURY · COUNCIL LIABLE

OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.front: Jacob, a gas engineer, is called to inspect a boiler in a house owned by Mr Feld. While inspecting the boiler, Jacob notices a structural crack in the adjoining wall. He leans against the wall to get a closer look at the boiler and the wall partially collapses, injuring him. Mr Feld was unaware of the crack. Advise Jacob.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.answer:
OLA 1957 — skilled visitor: Jacob is a lawful visitor. As a gas engineer, he is a skilled visitor exercising a particular calling.

s.2(3)(b) OLA 1957: An occupier may expect that a person in the exercise of their calling will appreciate and guard against special risks ordinarily incident to it. This provision could reduce Mr Feld's duty in respect of risks a gas engineer would normally expect to encounter.

**Key distinction:** The structural crack in the wall is not a risk ordinarily incident to the calling of a gas engineer. It is not connected with boiler inspection or the work Jacob was asked to do. s.2(3)(b) applies to risks specific to the trade — not to general structural dangers of the premises (Roles v Nathan [1963] CA — chimney sweeps' specialism covered gas/chimney risks, not other defects).

Occupier's liability: Mr Feld's duty to keep the premises reasonably safe extends to structural dangers that he knew or ought to have known about. If the crack was not visible and Feld had no knowledge, this may assist him — but the crack was visible to Jacob.

Conclusion: Jacob is likely entitled to recover. The wall defect falls outside the scope of s.2(3)(b). Mr Feld's lack of knowledge may reduce but not eliminate liability.
OLA 1957 — Special Categories of Visitor.summary_line: OLA 1957 · SKILLED VISITOR · S.2(3)(b) · ROLES v NATHAN · TRADE SPECIALISM · STRUCTURAL DEFECT · OUTSIDE SPECIALISM · OCCUPIER LIABLE

OLA 1957 — Exclusion and Independent Con.front: When will an occupier escape liability under the OLA 1957 for damage caused by an independent contractor working on the premises?
OLA 1957 — Exclusion and Independent Con.answer:
Under s.2(4)(b) OLA 1957, an occupier may avoid liability if:

1. It was reasonable to entrust the work to a contractor
2. The occupier took reasonable steps to satisfy themselves that the contractor was competent
3. The occupier inspected the work to the extent reasonably required.

For highly technical work, inspection is not required (Haseldine v Daw [1941] — lift maintenance). For non-technical work, the occupier should inspect (Woodward v Mayor of Hastings [1945] — icy step created by cleaner).
OLA 1957 — Exclusion and Independent Con.summary_line: INDEPENDENT CONTRACTOR · S.2(4)(b) OLA 1957 · REASONABLE TO ENTRUST · COMPETENT · INSPECTION · HASELDINE v DAW · WOODWARD

OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.front: Can an occupier exclude or restrict the duty of care owed to lawful visitors under the OLA 1957?
OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.answer:
Yes, in principle: s.2

1. OLA 1957 allows restriction or exclusion by agreement or notice, subject to:
1. Consumer Rights Act 2015 s.65 — where the occupier acts in the course of business and the visitor is a consumer (B2C), any term excluding liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence is void
2. Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 s.2
1. — applies to B2B business liability: exclusion of death or personal injury liability is void; other loss is subject to the reasonableness test (s.11 UCTA 1977).

⚠️ CRA 2015 governs B2C contracts (from October 2015); UCTA 1977 governs B2B business liability. Any notice must be brought to the visitor's attention before or at the time of entry.
OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.summary_line: EXCLUSION OF LIABILITY · OLA 1957 · CRA 2015 B2C · UCTA 1977 B2B · DEATH/PERSONAL INJURY CANNOT BE EXCLUDED · REASONABLENESS · ⚠️ CRA vs UCTA

OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.front: Nexo Gym Ltd operates a commercial gym. Members sign up via a smartphone app. The terms and conditions (displayed on screen during sign-up, with a tick-box) include the clause: 'Nexo Gym excludes all liability for any injury, including personal injury or death, caused by negligence.' Leon, a member, slips on a wet changing room floor and breaks his leg. Nexo Gym relies on the exclusion clause. Advise Leon.
OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.answer:
OLA 1957 and exclusion of liability:

Statutory control: Leon is a consumer (an individual member). Nexo Gym is a business. This is a B2C context.

Consumer Rights Act 2015 s.65: A trader cannot, by a term of a contract or by a notice, exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Any term purporting to do so is not binding on the consumer.

Effect on the clause: The clause purports to exclude liability for 'personal injury or death caused by negligence'. This is directly caught by CRA 2015 s.65. It is void and unenforceable against Leon.

Nexo Gym's position: The gym cannot rely on the exclusion clause. Leon's broken leg is personal injury caused by negligence (failure to maintain a dry changing room floor). The s.65 bar is absolute — it cannot be limited by the wording of the clause.

Conclusion: Leon can proceed with his OLA 1957 claim. The exclusion clause is void under CRA 2015 s.65. He must still prove the remaining elements of the claim (breach, causation, damage).
OLA 1957 — Exclusion of Liability.summary_line: OLA 1957 · EXCLUSION CLAUSE · CRA 2015 S.65 · B2C · PERSONAL INJURY · VOID · CANNOT EXCLUDE DEATH OR PERSONAL INJURY · GYM MEMBERSHIP

OLA 1984 — Trespassers.front: What duty, if any, does an occupier owe to trespassers under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1984?
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.answer:
Under s.1(3) OLA 1984, a duty arises only if all three conditions are satisfied:

1. The occupier knows of the danger or has reasonable grounds to believe it exists
2. The occupier knows or has reasonable grounds to believe that a trespasser is in or may come into the vicinity of the danger
3. The risk is one against which the occupier may reasonably be expected to offer some protection. If the duty arises, under s.1
4. the occupier must take reasonable care to see that the trespasser does not suffer injury by reason of the danger.

Under s.1(5), the duty can be discharged by taking reasonable steps to give warning or to discourage persons from incurring the risk.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.summary_line: OLA 1984 · TRESPASSERS · S.1(3) THREE CONDITIONS · KNOW OF DANGER · KNOW OF TRESPASSER · REASONABLE PROTECTION · S.1(5) DISCHARGE BY WARNING

OLA 1984 — Trespassers.front: What did Tomlinson v Congleton Borough Council [2003] HL decide about occupiers' liability to adult trespassers?
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.answer: Tomlinson v Congleton BC [2003] HL: Mr Tomlinson dived into a council lake (ignoring warning signs) and was seriously injured. The House of Lords held: the council was not liable under OLA 1984 — the risk of diving into shallow water was obvious and the claimant was fully aware of it. The duty under s.1(3) OLA 1984 did not arise, or was discharged by the warning signs. Lord Hoffmann emphasised that adults have personal responsibility for obvious risks, and occupiers should not be required to eliminate every risk to protect people from dangers arising from their own free choices.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.summary_line: TOMLINSON v CONGLETON · OLA 1984 · OBVIOUS RISK · ADULT TRESPASSER · PERSONAL RESPONSIBILITY · LORD HOFFMANN · NO DUTY

OLA 1984 — Trespassers.front:
Apply the OLA 1984 s.1(3) three-condition test to the following scenario:

A construction company is building a new housing development. The site is fenced off with hoarding, but local children regularly squeeze through a gap in the fence at night — site managers are aware of this. An eight-year-old child enters at night, falls into an uncovered foundation trench, and is seriously injured.

Apply each of the three s.1(3) conditions and reach a conclusion on liability.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.answer:
Under s.1(3) OLA 1984, a duty arises to a trespasser only if all three conditions are met:

Condition 1 — The occupier knows of the danger or has reasonable grounds to believe it exists:
An uncovered foundation trench on a construction site is plainly dangerous. The company knows it exists. Condition 1 is satisfied.

Condition 2 — The occupier knows or has reasonable grounds to believe a trespasser is in, or may come into, the vicinity of the danger:
Site managers are aware that children regularly enter through the gap. The company has reasonable grounds to believe trespassers (specifically children) are in the vicinity. Condition 2 is satisfied.

Condition 3 — The risk is one against which the occupier may reasonably be expected to offer some protection:
Children entering a building site at night face a serious risk from an uncovered trench. Given the company's knowledge of the access, it is reasonable to expect some protective steps. Condition 3 is likely satisfied.

Conclusion: All three conditions are met — a duty arises under s.1(4). Under s.1(5), the duty could have been discharged by adequate warning or by blocking the gap. Leaving the gap unfixed and the trench uncovered is unlikely to discharge the duty. The company is likely liable, subject to any contributory negligence adjustment for the child's age.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.summary_line: OLA 1984 · S.1(3) THREE CONDITIONS · CONSTRUCTION SITE · CHILD TRESPASSER · KNOWLEDGE OF DANGER · KNOWLEDGE OF TRESPASSERS · S.1(5) DISCHARGE

OLA 1984 — Trespassers.front: Tomlinson v Congleton Borough Council [2003] HL
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.issue: Whether a local authority owed a duty under OLA 1984 to an adult trespasser who dived into a lake on council land and was paralysed after striking the bottom.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.rule: Under s.1(3) OLA 1984, a duty to a trespasser arises only if all three conditions are met: the occupier knows of the danger; knows a trespasser may come into the vicinity; and the risk is one against which some protection might reasonably be expected. Where the risk is obvious and the claimant freely chose to engage with it, no duty arises or it is discharged. Adults bear personal responsibility for freely chosen obvious risks.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.application: Mr Tomlinson ignored warning signs and dived into a lake at a country park, striking the shallow bottom and suffering catastrophic injuries. He was a trespasser at the time of the dive. The HL held: the risk of diving into shallow water was obvious to any adult. The council owed no duty under OLA 1984 on these facts, and the warning signs would in any event have discharged any duty.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.conclusion: No liability. Lord Hoffmann delivered a powerful statement on personal responsibility: the law should not deprive the public of the enjoyment of open spaces to protect people from obvious risks of their own choosing. The case limits OLA 1984 duty significantly for adult trespassers facing obvious dangers.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.summary_line: OLA 1984 · TRESPASSER · OBVIOUS RISK · PERSONAL RESPONSIBILITY · ADULT DIVER · NO DUTY · LORD HOFFMANN · WARNING SIGNS SUFFICIENT

OLA 1984 — Trespassers.front: An abandoned warehouse on the edge of an industrial estate is owned by Harwick Properties Ltd. Local teenagers are known to use it for social gatherings; the owner is aware of this and has put up 'No Entry' signs but has not secured the premises. One evening, 17-year-old Tyler enters, falls through a rotten floorboard, and is seriously injured. Advise Tyler.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.answer:
OLA 1984 — trespasser: Tyler entered without permission. He is a trespasser.

s.1(3) OLA 1984 — three conditions:

1. Harwick knows of the danger or has reasonable grounds to believe it exists: Rotten floorboards in an abandoned warehouse are a known type of structural danger. If Harwick owns and maintains (or neglects) the premises, it has or should have knowledge of such dangers. Condition 1: likely satisfied.

2. Harwick knows or has reasonable grounds to believe trespassers may come into the vicinity: Harwick is aware local teenagers regularly use the premises. Condition 2: clearly satisfied.

3. The risk is one against which Harwick may reasonably be expected to offer some protection: Given known teenage access and structural dangers, some protective steps are reasonable.

Condition 3: likely satisfied.

Discharge under s.1(5): 'No Entry' signs were posted. Do these discharge the duty? Signs alone may be insufficient where the occupier knows they are being ignored and could take further steps (e.g., boarding up, fencing). Tomlinson v Congleton BC [2003] — signs sufficed for an obvious, self-chosen risk by an adult. Tyler is 17 and the danger (rotten floorboards) may not have been obvious to him.

Conclusion: Harwick is likely liable. Signs alone are unlikely to discharge the duty given actual knowledge of access and hidden structural danger.
OLA 1984 — Trespassers.summary_line: OLA 1984 · S.1(3) · THREE CONDITIONS · TRESPASSER · TEENAGER · ROTTEN FLOORBOARDS · KNOWN ACCESS · SIGNS INSUFFICIENT · S.1(5) · TOMLINSON DISTINGUISHED

Elements of Private Nuisance.front: What are the elements of the tort of private nuisance?
Elements of Private Nuisance.answer:
